COP | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$ | Only if $COP_R$ is Carnot | | 9 | VCRS — Refrigerating Effect | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | | 10 | VCRS — Compressor Work | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | | 11 | VCRS — Heat Rejected | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | | 12 | VCRS — COP | $COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | | 13 | Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_L}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | | 14 | Cylinder Volume (Compressor) | $V = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_{vol}}$ | m³/s | | 15 | Vapour Absorption — COP | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_{gen} + W_{pump}}$ | Dimensionless | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why study refrigeration in Process Utilities?** > - **Chemical Process Industries:** Cooling of reactors, condensation of vapours, crystallization, gas liquefaction (LNG, LPG), and cryogenic separations. > - **Pharmaceutical & Food:** Cold-chain storage, freeze-drying, preservation of biologicals. > - **HVAC:** Comfort air-conditioning, cold-room storage, ice plants. > - **Petrochemical:** Dew-point control of natural gas, LPG recovery, ethylene chilling trains. > - **Medical:** MRI superconductors, blood banks, vaccine storage at −70 °C. The choice between VCRS and VARS depends on the **availability of waste heat** versus **electrical power** at the plant site. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: COP of a Carnot Refrigerator Consider a reversible (Carnot) refrigeration cycle operating between a cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$ (in Kelvin) and a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$. **Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cyclic device:** $$ \oint dQ = \oint dW $$ Since the cycle returns to its initial state, the change in internal energy is zero, so the net heat transfer equals the net work transfer. $$ Q_H - Q_L = W_{net} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Second Law for a reversible cycle:** For a reversible cycle, the cyclic integral of $\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$ is zero: $$ \oint \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 $$ Expanding over the four reversible processes: $$ \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0 $$ This gives us the fundamental entropy balance: $$ \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} $$ **Step 3 — Express $Q_H$ in terms of $Q_L$:** $$ Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the work expression:** $$ W_{net} = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Form the COP ratio:** $$ COP_{C,R} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}} $$ The $Q_L$ terms cancel: $$ COP_{C,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} $$ **Step 6 — Derive the corresponding Heat Pump COP:** Using $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$, and substituting $Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$: $$ COP_{C,HP} = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}}{Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)} $$ $$ COP_{C,HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} $$ ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: COP of a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) A standard VCRS uses a saturated vapour at the compressor inlet (state 1), which undergoes: - **1 → 2:** Isentropic compression in the compressor. - **2 → 3:** Constant-pressure heat rejection in the condenser (desuperheating + condensation + subcooling) to saturated liquid. - **3 → 4:** Throttling (isenthalpic expansion) through the expansion valve. - **4 → 1:** Constant-pressure evaporation in the evaporator. **Step 1 — Energy balance on the Evaporator (control volume 4→1):** Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant: $$ q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} $$ **Step 2 — Energy balance on the Compressor (control volume 1→2):** For an ideal (isentropic) compressor, neglecting kinetic and potential energy: $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} $$ **Step 3 — Energy balance on the Condenser (control volume 2→3):** $$ q_H = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} $$ **Step 4 — Energy balance on the Expansion Valve (control volume 3→4):** For an isenthalpic throttling process: $$ h_4 = h_3 $$ This is the classic **Joule–Thomson throttling** relation. **Step 5 — Formulate the COP:** $$ COP_{VCRS} = \dfrac{\text{Refrigerating Effect}}{\text{Compressor Work}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Step 6 — Mass flow rate of refrigerant for a given cooling load $\dot{Q}_L$:** $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} \quad \text{[kg/s]} $$ **Step 7 — Compressor power required:** $$ \dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{[kW]} $$ **Step 8 — Theoretical cylinder displacement of compressor (swept volume per second):** $$ \dot{V}_{swept} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_{vol}} $$ where $v_1$ is the specific volume of refrigerant vapour at the compressor inlet, and $\eta_{vol}$ is the volumetric efficiency. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Refrigeration Systems | Parameter | Carnot Cycle (Ideal) | VCRS (Practical) | VARS (Practical) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Driver** | Reversible work | Mechanical compressor | Heat + small pump | | **COP (typical)** | Highest (limit) | 2.5 – 4.5 | 0.3 – 0.7 | | **Moving parts** | 0 (theoretical) | 1 (compressor) | Few (pump only) | | **Noise level** | None | High | Very low | | **Maintenance** | None | High | Low | | **Suitable for** | Theoretical benchmark | All scales; especially large cooling loads | Sites with abundant waste heat | | **Refrigerant type** | Any (theoretical) | Halocarbons, hydrocarbons, ammonia | Ammonia–water, Lithium bromide–water | | **Energy input form** | Work | Work (electric) | Heat (thermal) | ## 3.4 Numerical Demonstration — Sample Problem > **Problem:** A refrigerator operates between −10 °C and 35 °C. It removes 5 kW of heat from the cold space. Find: (a) COP if it were a Carnot refrigerator, (b) actual COP if the actual compressor work is 2.5 kW, (c) rate of heat rejection. **Solution:** Convert to Kelvin: $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263\ K$, $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308\ K$. **(a) Carnot COP:** $$ COP_{C,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{263}{308 - 263} = \dfrac{263}{45} $$ $$ COP_{C,R} = 5.844 $$ **(b) Actual COP:** $$ COP_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{5}{2.5} = 2.0 $$ **(c) Rate of heat rejection:** From the First Law: $$ \dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net} = 5 + 2.5 = 7.5\ kW $$ > **Interpretation:** The actual COP (2.0) is significantly lower than the Carnot limit (5.844). The ratio $\dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \dfrac{2.0}{5.844} = 34.2\%$ is the **Second-Law Efficiency** or **Exergy efficiency** of the refrigerator. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Diagram: Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRS) > [!NOTE] > The diagram below is a **block-level functional flow** depicting the four essential components and the state-points of the working fluid, as it would appear on a standard KTU Process Utilities worksheet. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[STATE 1
Saturated Vapour
Low Pressure P1
Temp TL] --> B1[COMPRESSOR
Isentropic Compression
Wc h2 minus h1] B1 --> A2[STATE 2
Superheated Vapour
High Pressure P2
Temp TH] A2 --> C1[CONDENSER
Constant Pressure Heat Rejection
QH h2 minus h3] C1 --> A3[STATE 3
Saturated Liquid
High Pressure P2
Temp TH] A3 --> D1[EXPANSION VALVE
Isenthalpic Throttling
h4 equals h3] D1 --> A4[STATE 4
Liquid Vapour Mixture
Low Pressure P1
Temp TL] A4 --> E1[EVAPORATOR
Constant Pressure Heat Absorption
QL h1 minus h4] E1 --> A1 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic Diagram: Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[EVAPORATOR
Low Pressure
Refrigerant absorbs QL] --> B1[ABSORBER
Refrigerant vapour
absorbed by absorbent] B1 --> C1[PUMP
Liquid solution
pumped to high pressure
Wpump] C1 --> D1[GENERATOR
Heat input Qgen
drives refrigerant out] D1 --> E1[CONDENSER
Refrigerant condenses
rejecting QH] E1 --> F1[EXPANSION VALVE
Pressure reduced
isenthalpic] F1 --> A1 D1 --> G1[PRESSURE REDUCING VALVE
Weak solution returns
to absorber] G1 --> B1 ``` ## 4.3 Refrigeration Systems Classification Block ```mermaid flowchart TB R[REFRIGERATION SYSTEMS] R --> M1[Mechanical Compression VCRS] R --> M2[Thermal Absorption VARS] R --> M3[Jet Steam Systems] R --> M4[Thermoelectric Peltier] M1 --> S1[Reciprocating Compressor] M1 --> S2[Centrifugal Compressor] M1 --> S3[Rotary Screw Compressor] M2 --> S4[Ammonia Water System] M2 --> S5[LiBr Water System] M3 --> S6[Steam Jet Ejector] M4 --> S7[Semiconductor Peltier Modules] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Refrigerant Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB Q[Required Refrigeration Effect] Q --> A{Temperature Range} A -->|Above 0 Degree C| B[HCFC HFC Options] A -->|Between minus 20 and 0| C[Ammonia R717 Hydrocarbons] A -->|Below minus 20 Degree C| D[Cascade System or Cascade Refrigerants] B --> E[Evaluate GWP ODP Toxicity Flammability] C --> E D --> E E --> F[Final Refrigerant Selection] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define: (i) Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator, (ii) Ton of Refrigeration.** **Model Answer:** (i) **COP of a Refrigerator:** The Coefficient of Performance is defined as the ratio of the rate of heat removed from the cold space (desired effect) to the rate of work input required to accomplish this removal. $$ COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} $$ It is a dimensionless quantity. For a Carnot refrigerator, $COP_{C,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$. (ii) **Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** It is a unit of cooling capacity. One ton of refrigeration is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze one ton (2000 lb or 907.185 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. $$ 1\ TR = \dfrac{907.185 \times 335}{24 \times 3600}\ kW \approx 3.517\ kW $$ where 335 kJ/kg is the latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State the difference between a refrigerator and a heat pump.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Refrigerator | Heat Pump | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Objective** | To maintain a temperature $T_L$ lower than ambient | To maintain a temperature $T_H$ higher than ambient | | **Useful output** | Cooling effect $Q_L$ | Heating effect $Q_H$ | | **COP definition** | $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net}$ | $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W_{net}$ | | **Relation** | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Same cycle, just different point of interest | | **Example** | Domestic fridge, AC unit, cold storage | Room heater in winter, water geyser in winter | | **Operating range** | $T_L < T_{ambient}$ | $T_H > T_{ambient}$ | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`** **(a)** Derive an expression for the COP of a Carnot refrigerator operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_L$ and $T_H$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS). Also derive an expression for its COP. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation of Carnot COP:** The Carnot refrigeration cycle operates reversibly between two reservoirs at temperatures $T_L$ and $T_H$. Applying the **First Law** to the cycle: $$ Q_H - Q_L = W_{net} $$ Applying the **Second Law** (reversible cycle, total entropy change is zero): $$ \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0 $$ From this, $Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$. Substituting into the work equation: $$ W_{net} = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} $$ Therefore, the COP is: $$ COP_{C,R} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} $$ **Part (b) — VCRS Working and COP:** The VCRS consists of four main components: **compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator**, interconnected in a closed loop with the refrigerant circulating through them. **Process 1 → 2 (Compression):** Saturated vapour at low pressure $P_1$ enters the compressor and is compressed isentropically to high pressure $P_2$. The refrigerant becomes superheated. **Process 2 → 3 (Condensation):** The hot, superheated vapour enters the condenser where it rejects heat $Q_H$ to the surroundings at constant pressure. It first desuperheats, then condenses to saturated liquid, and is often subcooled. **Process 3 → 4 (Throttling):** The high-pressure saturated liquid passes through an expansion valve (or capillary tube). This is an **isenthalpic** process — enthalpy remains constant: $h_4 = h_3$. **Process 4 → 1 (Evaporation):** The low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture enters the evaporator, where it absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold space at constant pressure, becoming saturated vapour again at state 1. **Energy balances per kg of refrigerant:** $$ q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad ; \quad w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad ; \quad q_H = h_2 - h_3 $$ **COP of VCRS:** $$ \boxed{COP_{VCRS} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}} $$ **Numerical Estimation:** For R-134a in a typical domestic refrigerator, $h_1 \approx 247\ kJ/kg$, $h_2 \approx 270\ kJ/kg$, $h_3 = h_4 \approx 95\ kJ/kg$. $$ COP = \dfrac{247 - 95}{270 - 247} = \dfrac{152}{23} \approx 6.6 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that the **expansion valve is isenthalpic**, NOT isentropic. There is NO work output and entropy INCREASES across this device. Writing $h_4 < h_3$ will cost you 2 marks. Also, in VCRS the refrigerant leaves the evaporator as **saturated vapour (state 1)**, not as a wet mixture — this is by design to prevent liquid slugging at the compressor inlet. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`** **(a)** Explain the working of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS). Compare it with VCRS. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant. Mention the environmental concerns of CFCs and the role of HFCs. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — VARS Working:** A Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System uses **heat energy** instead of mechanical work to produce the cooling effect. The most common working pair is **ammonia (refrigerant) – water (absorbent)**. Other pairs include **Lithium Bromide – water** (for AC above 0 °C). **Major Components:** 1. **Evaporator** — Refrigerant absorbs heat $Q_L$ at low pressure and evaporates. 2. **Absorber** — Ammonia vapour is absorbed by weak aqua-ammonia solution, forming a strong aqua-ammonia solution. Heat $Q_A$ is rejected here. 3. **Pump** — The strong solution is pumped to the generator. Pump work $W_p$ is small. 4. **Generator** — Heat $Q_{gen}$ (often waste heat or steam) is supplied. The ammonia vapour is driven out of the solution. 5. **Condenser** — Ammonia vapour is condensed at high pressure, rejecting $Q_H$ to the surroundings. 6. **Expansion Valve** — Liquid ammonia is throttled to the low evaporator pressure. **Working Principle:** Unlike VCRS which uses a compressor, VARS uses an **absorber–generator combination** to achieve compression. The ammonia is "chemically compressed" by being absorbed and then thermally driven out. **COP of VARS:** $$ COP_{VARS} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_{gen} + W_p} $$ Because $W_p$ is very small: $$ COP_{VARS} \approx \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_{gen}} $$ **Comparison VARS vs VCRS:** | Feature | VARS | VCRS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Energy input** | Heat (thermal) | Mechanical work (electric) | | **Moving parts** | Only pump; no compressor | Compressor (reciprocating/rotary) | | **Noise** | Silent | Noisy | | **COP** | Low (0.3 – 0.7) | High (2.5 – 6.0) | | **Best suited for** | Locations with cheap/waste heat | Standard cooling applications | | **Equipment cost** | Higher initial | Lower initial | | **Refrigerant** | Ammonia (with water absorbent) | R-12, R-134a, R-22, R-717, R-290 | | **Maintenance** | Complex absorber/generator | Simpler; mainly compressor | **Part (b) — Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant:** A refrigerant must satisfy the following **thermodynamic, chemical, and environmental** criteria: **Thermodynamic Properties:** - Low boiling point at desired evaporator pressure. - Low specific heat of liquid (smaller refrigerant mass flow). - High latent heat of vaporisation (large cooling effect per kg). - Low specific volume of vapour (smaller compressor size). - Low viscosity (better heat transfer, smaller pumping losses). - High thermal conductivity (better heat exchange). **Chemical Properties:** - Chemically stable — does not decompose in the system. - Non-corrosive to metals (copper, steel, aluminium). - Non-flammable and non-explosive. - Non-toxic to humans. - Miscible with compressor lubricating oil (or ability to use appropriate oil). **Environmental Properties:** - Zero **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)**. - Low **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**. - Short atmospheric lifetime. - Recyclable and reusable. **CFC Concerns and HFC Role:** **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** — e.g., R-11, R-12 — contain chlorine atoms which are released into the stratosphere, catalytically destroying the **ozone layer** via the Rowland–Molina cycle. Each chlorine atom can destroy up to **100,000 ozone molecules**. This led to the **1987 Montreal Protocol**, which mandated a phase-out of CFCs. **Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs)** — e.g., R-22 — were transitional replacements with low (but non-zero) ODP. **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** — e.g., R-134a, R-410A — contain no chlorine, hence **zero ODP**. However, many HFCs have **high GWP** and are now regulated under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol. **Emerging Alternatives:** - **Hydrocarbons (HC):** R-290 (propane), R-600a (isobutane) — zero ODP, negligible GWP, but flammable. - **Natural refrigerants:** Ammonia (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), water (R-718). - **HFOs (Hydrofluoroolefins):** R-1234yf — ultra-low GWP. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **ODP** and **GWP**. Remember: **ODP** is about ozone destruction (CFCs have high ODP, HFCs have zero); **GWP** is about climate warming (some HFCs like R-134a have GWP ≈ 1430 — much worse than CO₂ itself!). Writing "HFCs are completely safe" will lose 2 marks. Always mention both ODP and GWP for a complete answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Refrigeration** - **Refrigeration** = Heat transfer from a low-temperature body to a high-temperature body, against the natural gradient, using external work or heat. - **Refrigerator vs Heat Pump:** Same cycle; refrigerator values $Q_L$, heat pump values $Q_H$. The relation $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ **always** holds for cycles operating between two reservoirs. - **Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** $1\ TR = 3.517\ kW = 211\ kJ/min$. - **COP is dimensionless** and is the ratio of the desired effect to the work input. Higher COP = more efficient cycle. - **Carnot COP limits:** $COP_{C,R} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ and $COP_{C,HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$. **All real cycles operate below the Carnot COP.** - **Temperature in COP formula MUST be in Kelvin** — converting from °C is a common mistake. - **VCRS Components:** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → (back to Compressor). - **Expansion Valve is isenthalpic ($h_4 = h_3$), NOT isentropic** — entropy increases; this is a key KTU trap. - **VCRS COP formula:** $COP_{VCRS} = (h_1 - h_4) / (h_2 - h_1)$ with $h_3 = h_4$. - **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}_L / (h_1 - h_4)$; Compressor power: $\dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$. - **VARS uses heat, not mechanical work**; VARS COP is much lower than VCRS COP but uses waste heat effectively. - **Ammonia (R-717):** Excellent thermodynamic properties but toxic; standard for large industrial plants. - **Water (R-718):** Used in LiBr–water absorption systems for AC above 0 °C. - **R-134a:** Standard automotive/domestic refrigerant; zero ODP, but high GWP. - **R-744 (CO₂):** Natural refrigerant with very low GWP, gaining popularity in transcritical cycles. - **Montreal Protocol (1987)** — phased out CFCs; **Kigali Amendment (2016)** — phasedown of high-GWP HFCs. - **Second-Law Efficiency (Exergy Efficiency):** $\eta_{II} = COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot}$; a measure of how close the actual cycle is to the reversible ideal. - **Heat rejected at condenser:** Always $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net}$ (energy conservation). - **For exam numericals:** Always draw the P–h or T–s diagram, label all four state points, mark $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ explicitly, and apply the energy-balance equations in the correct order.

topics.title: Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Refrigeration?

**Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body/reservoir and transferring it to a high-temperature body/reservoir, against the natural flow of heat (i.e., against the Second Law of Thermodynamics). A machine/cycle used to accomplish this continuous heat transfer from a cold region to a hot region using external work or heat input is called a **Refrigeration Cycle** or **Refrigerator**.

The working substance that absorbs heat at low temperature and rejects it at high temperature is called the **Refrigerant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Refrigeration is defined as the process of producing and maintaining a temperature below that of the surroundings in a given space or substance.* The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of desired effect (refrigeration effect) to the work input required.

Mathematically, the desired figure of merit is:

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{\text{Refrigeration Effect (RE)}}{\text{Work Input (W}_{net}\text{)}}
$$

## 1.2 Classification of Refrigeration Cycles (KTU Module 4 Scope)

| Cycle Family | Energy Input | Working Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vapour Compression (VCR)** | Mechanical Work (Compressor) | Phase change of refrigerant (vapor compression) | Domestic refrigerator, AC |
| **Vapour Absorption (VAR)** | Heat Energy (Generator) | Absorption + desorption of vapor in a liquid | Industrial chillers, solar cooling |
| Gas Refrigeration | Mechanical Work | Reversible compression/expansion of a gas | Air cycle, Bell Coleman |
| Thermoelectric | Electrical Energy | Peltier effect | Portable coolers |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Module 4** of OECHT611, the focus is exclusively on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR) cycle** and the **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration (VAR) cycle**, including their components, P-h / T-s representations, derivations, and COP comparison.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "Refrigeration = A Heat Pump Working Backwards"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Towel & Bucket Analogy:**
> Imagine a wet towel sitting in a cool room. To dry it, you carry water *uphill* from the towel (cold) to a bucket placed on a high shelf (hot) — this is unnatural, so you must spend energy. A refrigerator does the same: it carries heat *uphill* from a cold interior (the towel) to a hot room (the bucket) using energy.
>
> - **VCR** = A man manually pumping water up a pipe (uses **mechanical work** from a compressor).
> - **VAR** = A wick inside the pipe that soaks up the water at the bottom and evaporates it at the top using **heat from a flame** (no pump, just thermal energy).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Refrigerants (Must-Know Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Standard refrigeration ton (TR)** | **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** |
| **COP (range)** | 2 to 7 for VCR systems |
| **Carnot COP (refrigerator)** | $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ |
| **Common Refrigerants** | R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-717 (Ammonia), R-502 |
| **Absorbent-Refrigerant Pairs** | LiBr–H₂O (chillers), NH₃–H₂O (cold storage) |
| **Reference State (IIR)** | h = 200 kJ/kg, s = 1 kJ/kg·K at 0 °C saturated liquid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR) Cycle

### 2.1.1 Definition
The **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle** is a closed, mechanical-work-driven cycle in which a refrigerant undergoes a **phase change** (vaporisation–condensation) to absorb heat at low temperature and reject it at high temperature. The refrigerant always **remains in the two-phase region** during heat exchange.

### 2.1.2 Main Components

1. **Compressor** — Rises the pressure of low-pressure vapour refrigerant to high pressure. Input = **mechanical work (Wc)**.
2. **Condenser** — High-pressure, high-temperature vapour rejects heat (QH) to the surroundings and condenses to liquid.
3. **Expansion Valve (Throttle)** — Liquid refrigerant is expanded isenthalpically from high pressure to low pressure (partially flashing to vapour).
4. **Evaporator** — Low-pressure, low-temperature liquid–vapour mixture absorbs heat (QL) from the refrigerated space and fully vaporises.

### 2.1.3 The Four Ideal Processes (Standard VCR Cycle)

| Process | State Change | Property Constant | Heat/Work |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 → 2** | Compression | **Isentropic (s₁ = s₂)** | Wc = h₂ − h₁ (work in) |
| **2 → 3** | Condensation | **Isobaric (P₂ = P₃)** | QH = h₂ − h₃ (heat out) |
| **3 → 4** | Expansion | **Isenthalpic (h₃ = h₄)** | q = 0 (throttling) |
| **4 → 1** | Evaporation | **Isobaric (P₄ = P₁)** | QL = h₁ − h₄ (heat in) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Assumptions for Ideal VCR Cycle:**
> 1. Compression is **isentropic** (reversible + adiabatic).
> 2. Refrigerant leaves the **condenser as saturated liquid** (no sub-cooling).
> 3. Refrigerant leaves the **evaporator as saturated vapour** (no superheating).
> 4. Pressure drops in the lines are negligible.
> 5. Throttling is isenthalpic.

## 2.2 The Vapour Absorption Refrigeration (VAR) Cycle

### 2.2.1 Definition
The **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycle** is a heat-driven refrigeration cycle where the mechanical compressor of VCR is replaced by an **Absorber + Pump + Generator** assembly. The refrigerant vapour is absorbed in a liquid absorbent, pumped to high pressure, and then desorbed (boiled off) by supplying heat.

### 2.2.2 Main Components

1. **Evaporator** — Same as VCR; refrigerant absorbs QL.
2. **Absorber** — Vapour refrigerant is absorbed by weak solution → strong liquid solution.
3. **Pump** — Strong solution is pumped to high pressure (small work input Wp).
4. **Generator (Boiler)** — Heat Qgen is supplied to desorb refrigerant vapour.
5. **Condenser** — High-pressure vapour rejects heat QH and condenses.
6. **Expansion Valve** — Liquid expanded to evaporator pressure.

### 2.2.3 Common Working Pairs

| Pair | Refrigerant | Absorbent | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NH₃–H₂O** | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | Industrial / cold storage |
| **LiBr–H₂O** | Water (H₂O) | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) | A/C chillers (T > 0 °C) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **NH₃–H₂O system**, **NH₃ is the refrigerant** and **H₂O is the absorbent**. The high-pressure side contains NH₃ vapour + H₂O vapour, so the vapour leaving the generator must be **rectified (purified)** before entering the condenser.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas use SI units (kJ/kg, kW, °C / K).** Always work in **kJ/kg** for specific quantities; multiply by **ṁ (kg/s)** to get kW.

### VCR Cycle Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | in kJ/kg |
| Compressor Work | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | in kJ/kg |
| Heat Rejected in Condenser | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | in kJ/kg |
| **COP of VCR** | $\text{COP}_{VCR} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_L \, (\text{kW})}{h_1 - h_4 \, (\text{kJ/kg})}$ | kg/s |
| Power Required | $\dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | kW |
| Volume Flow Rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{60}$ | m³/min |
| Power per TR | $\dfrac{3.517}{\text{COP}}$ | kW/TR |
| Carnot COP (Ref.) | $\text{COP}_{C} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless |

### VAR Cycle Formulas

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| **COP of VAR (ideal)** | $\text{COP}_{VAR} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_8}{(h_5 - h_4) + (h_7 - h_6)}$ |
| Approximate COP (if Wp ≈ 0) | $\text{COP}_{VAR} \approx \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_{gen}}$ |
| Generator Heat | $Q_{gen} = h_5 + (h_7 - h_6) - h_4$ |
| Heat at Absorber | $Q_A = h_1 + h_6 - h_8$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **Carnot COP of a refrigerator** $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ with the **Carnot COP of a heat pump** $T_H/(T_H - T_L)$. The signs and the **purpose** of the device are different.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **VCR:** Used in 95 % of household refrigerators, air-conditioners, cold-chain logistics, frozen food transport, cryogenic gas liquefaction (multi-stage), and chemical process cooling.
- **VAR:** Used where waste heat or solar thermal energy is abundant — e.g., large industrial chillers, food preservation in rural off-grid setups, district cooling systems. It can be powered by low-grade heat, making it ideal for **energy recovery applications**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of COP for the Ideal VCR Cycle

### Starting Point — First Law Applied to Each Component

We apply the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each control volume. Neglecting KE and PE:

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_{out} - h_{in})
$$

#### (a) Evaporator (Process 4 → 1) — Heat Absorbed = Refrigeration Effect

The refrigerant absorbs heat at constant low pressure. No work is done.

$$
q_L = h_1 - h_4
$$

This is the **Refrigeration Effect per unit mass (RE)**.

#### (b) Compressor (Process 1 → 2) — Isentropic Compression

Compressor work input, assuming adiabatic and reversible (isentropic):

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1
$$

Energy balance:

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad (\text{since } q = 0, \, s_1 = s_2)
$$

#### (c) Condenser (Process 2 → 3) — Heat Rejected

The refrigerant rejects heat at constant high pressure. No work is done.

$$
q_H = h_2 - h_3
$$

#### (d) Expansion Valve (Process 3 → 4) — Throttling

For an isenthalpic throttle, energy balance gives:

$$
h_3 = h_4
$$

(no work, no heat, negligible KE/PE change)

### 3.2 Final Derivation — COP of VCR

By definition, **COP of a refrigerator = Desired cooling ÷ Work input**.

$$
\text{COP}_{VCR} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

Substituting $h_4 = h_3$ (throttling):

$$
\text{COP}_{VCR} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the ideal VCR cycle. To solve numerical problems, the enthalpies $h_1, h_2, h_3$ are obtained from the **refrigerant property tables** (saturation tables + superheated tables).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of COP for the Ideal VAR Cycle (8-State Notation)

We follow the **8-state standard notation** used universally in KTU textbooks:

| State | Location | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Evaporator exit | Saturated vapour (refrigerant) |
| 2 | Absorber exit / Pump inlet | Strong liquid solution |
| 3 | Pump exit | Strong liquid solution (high P) |
| 4 | Generator inlet (after heat exchange) | Strong solution |
| 5 | Generator exit (vapour) | Refrigerant + absorbent vapour |
| 6 | Strong solution return to absorber | Strong solution (high P) |
| 7 | Refrigerant vapour to condenser | Pure refrigerant |
| 8 | After condenser + expansion valve | Liquid refrigerant at low P |

> [!NOTE]
> In the simplified **5-component VAR** model, the generator exit vapour is often assumed to be **pure refrigerant** (state 7), and the strong solution returns directly to the absorber. This simplifies the COP to:

$$
\text{COP}_{VAR} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_{gen} + W_p} = \frac{\dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_8)}{\dot{m}_r (h_5 - h_4) + \dot{m}_s (h_7 - h_6)}
$$

For most **KTU exam problems**, the pump work is small and the standard simplified expression used is:

$$
\text{COP}_{VAR} = \frac{h_1 - h_8}{(h_5 - h_4) + (h_7 - h_6)}
$$

where $(h_5 - h_4)$ is the **heat input at the generator** and $(h_7 - h_6)$ is the energy associated with returning the strong solution.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **[Sample Problem — 7 Marks]**
> An ideal VCR cycle uses R-134a as refrigerant. The evaporator temperature is −10 °C and the condenser temperature is 40 °C. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid and the compressor as superheated vapour. Determine:
> (a) COP of the cycle
> (b) Refrigerating effect in kJ/kg
> (c) Mass flow rate required for a 10 TR plant

**Given (from R-134a property tables):**
- At −10 °C (sat. vapour): h₁ = 392.3 kJ/kg, s₁ = 1.7335 kJ/kg·K
- At 40 °C (sat. liquid): h₃ = 256.5 kJ/kg
- At 40 °C, s_g = 0.6948 kJ/kg·K; superheated table at 40 °C, P = 1.0 MPa gives h₂ ≈ 430.4 kJ/kg at s = 1.7335 kJ/kg·K
- h₄ = h₃ = 256.5 kJ/kg (throttling)

#### Step (a) — COP Calculation

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{392.3 - 256.5}{430.4 - 392.3}
$$

$$
= \frac{135.8}{38.1} = 3.564
$$

#### Step (b) — Refrigeration Effect

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392.3 - 256.5 = 135.8 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

#### Step (c) — Mass Flow Rate for 10 TR

$$
1 \; \text{TR} = 3.517 \; \text{kW} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_L = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_L}{RE} = \frac{35.17}{135.8} = 0.259 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

#### Final Answers

- **COP = 3.56**
- **RE = 135.8 kJ/kg**
- **ṁ = 0.259 kg/s**

> [!TIP]
> **Valuator's Trick:** In the exam, **always show the table reference (or property values)** that you used. KTU examiners give partial credit even if the final number is slightly off, **provided your property lookup step is shown explicitly**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — VCR vs. VAR (KTU Favourite)

| Feature | Vapour Compression (VCR) | Vapour Absorption (VAR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Prime Mover** | Compressor (mechanical work) | Generator (heat input) |
| **Energy Input** | High-grade (electricity / shaft work) | Low-grade (waste heat, solar) |
| **Moving Parts** | Many (compressor piston, valves) | Almost none in primary loop |
| **Noise** | High | Very low (silent operation) |
| **COP Range** | 2 to 7 | 0.3 to 0.7 (lower than VCR) |
| **Capacity Range** | Small to medium (W to MW) | Medium to very large (TR to 1000+ TR) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent (compressor wear) | Low |
| **Refrigerant Purity** | Single, pure refrigerant | Working pair (mixture) → rectification often needed |
| **Best Application** | Household, transport AC | Industrial chillers, solar cooling |
| **Capacity Modulation** | Easy (compressor speed/on-off) | Difficult (heat input modulation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (2024 Scheme):** When asked "compare VCR and VAR", always present the answer as a **two-column table** and include **at least 5 distinct comparison points**. Marks are awarded for **clarity and coverage**, not just content.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — VCR Cycle Layout (Block-Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Compressor\n1 to 2] -->|Wc| A2[Condenser\n2 to 3]
    A2 -->|QH rejected| A3[Expansion Valve\n3 to 4]
    A3 -->|h constant| A4[Evaporator\n4 to 1]
    A4 -->|QL absorbed| A1
    A2 -.Heat out.-> OUT((Surroundings\nHot))
    A4 -.Heat in.-> COLD((Refrigerated\nSpace))
```

> Node IDs are alphanumeric (A1–A4) to comply with Mermaid parser; labels use plain uppercase text.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — P-h Diagram Representation (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Dome["Saturation Dome"]
        direction TB
        L1["Sat. Liquid Line (h3)"] --- V1["Sat. Vapour Line (h1)"]
    end
    P1["1: Sat. Vapour\nLow P, Low T"] -->|Isentropic Compression| P2["2: Superheated Vapour\nHigh P, High T"]
    P2 -->|Isobaric Condensation| P3["3: Sat. Liquid\nHigh P"]
    P3 -->|Isenthalpic Throttling| P4["4: Two-phase Mixture\nLow P, h4 = h3"]
    P4 -->|Isobaric Evaporation| P1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — VAR Cycle Component Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Evaporator\n1 to 8] -->|QL absorbed| B2[Absorber\nVapour to Liquid]
    B2 -->|Strong Solution| B3[Pump\n2 to 3]
    B3 -->|Wp| B4[Generator\n3 to 5 and 7]
    B4 -->|Qgen in| B5[Condenser\n7 to 8]
    B5 -->|QH out| B1
    B4 -->|Weak Solution| B2
    B4 -.Refrigerant Vapour.-> B5
```

> **Note:** In the NH₃–H₂O VAR, the vapour leaving the generator contains a small amount of water, so a **rectifier** is added between the generator and condenser to remove water. In the LiBr–H₂O system, the refrigerant is water itself, so the rectifier is unnecessary.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Comparative Architecture (VCR vs. VAR)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VCR["VCR System"]
        VC1[Compressor] --> VC2[Condenser]
        VC2 --> VC3[Expansion Valve]
        VC3 --> VC4[Evaporator]
        VC4 --> VC1
    end
    subgraph VAR["VAR System"]
        VA1[Generator] --> VA2[Condenser]
        VA2 --> VA3[Expansion Valve]
        VA3 --> VA4[Evaporator]
        VA4 --> VA5[Absorber]
        VA5 --> VA6[Pump]
        VA6 --> VA1
    end
    VCR -.Uses.-> WK1[Mechanical Work Input]
    VAR -.Uses.-> WK2[Heat Input from Generator]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from the COP of a heat pump?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect to the work input required to achieve it.
>
> $$\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{\text{Refrigeration Effect } (Q_L)}{\text{Work Input } (W_{net})}$$
>
> A **heat pump**, on the other hand, delivers heat to the hot space. Its COP is the ratio of the desired heating effect to the work input.
>
> $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{\text{Heating Effect } (Q_H)}{\text{Work Input } (W_{net})}$$
>
> The key relationship is:
>
> $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_{R} + 1$$
>
> The refrigerator aims to **extract** heat (cooling), while the heat pump aims to **deliver** heat (heating). Both operate on the same thermodynamic cycle but have different "desired outputs."

*(3 marks — clear definition + 1 numerical relationship + difference)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the function of (i) an expansion valve and (ii) a condenser in a VCR cycle.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Expansion Valve (Throttle):** Its function is to reduce the pressure of the condensed liquid refrigerant from the **high condenser pressure** to the **low evaporator pressure**. This is achieved through an **isenthalpic (throttling) process**, where $h_3 = h_4$. A small portion of the liquid flashes into vapour, and the resulting two-phase mixture enters the evaporator.
>
> **(ii) Condenser:** Its function is to **reject the latent heat of condensation (QH)** of the high-pressure, high-temperature refrigerant vapour to the surroundings. The refrigerant enters as a superheated vapour and leaves as a saturated liquid (or sub-cooled liquid) at constant pressure.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for correct function with associated process state)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** write the units of enthalpy as J/kg if the COP is dimensionless — COP is **always unitless**.
> - When you write a P-h or T-s diagram, **label every state point (1, 2, 3, 4)** and the direction of processes. A diagram without state labels fetches **0 marks** for that sub-part.
> - **Show the energy balance equation for each component**, not just the final formula. Partial credit is given for the SFEE application step.
> - In numerical problems, **always state the property values** you used (or reference the table) — this protects you against deduction for "unsupported numbers."

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**A.** (a) Draw the schematic of a **Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR) cycle** and represent it on a **Pressure–Enthalpy (P-h) diagram**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A VCR cycle operates between an evaporator temperature of **−15 °C** and a condenser temperature of **30 °C**. The refrigerant is **R-134a**, and the compression is isentropic. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid and the evaporator as saturated vapour. Using the property values given below, calculate the **COP** of the cycle, the **refrigeration effect (RE)** in kJ/kg, and the **compressor work** in kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Property data (R-134a):**
- At −15 °C, sat. vapour: $h_1 = 389.2 \; \text{kJ/kg}, \; s_1 = 1.7395 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- At 30 °C, sat. liquid: $h_3 = 241.5 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- At 30 °C, 0.77 MPa (superheated): at $s = 1.7395$, $h_2 = 425.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Schematic & P-h Diagram (7 marks)**

**Schematic (Block layout):**

A block diagram of the VCR cycle must contain the four components — Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator — connected in a closed loop, with the direction of refrigerant flow indicated by arrows. State points 1, 2, 3, 4 must be marked at the entry/exit of each component.

**P-h Diagram Description:**

On a P-h diagram, the cycle is represented inside the **saturation dome** of the refrigerant.

- **Point 1**: Inside the dome, on the saturated vapour line (low pressure, low temperature).
- **Point 2**: Outside the dome, above and to the right of point 1 (high pressure, superheated vapour).
- **Point 3**: On the saturated liquid line at high pressure (vertical drop from 2 to 3 along the constant-pressure line).
- **Point 4**: Inside the dome, on the same constant-pressure (low) line as point 1, directly to the left of point 1 (a vertical drop from 3 to 4 — this is the throttling line, since h₄ = h₃).

**Processes:**
- **1 → 2**: Isentropic compression (vertical line on P-h, going up)
- **2 → 3**: Isobaric condensation (horizontal line going left, with phase change)
- **3 → 4**: Isenthalpic expansion (vertical line going down, partially crossing the dome)
- **4 → 1**: Isobaric evaporation (horizontal line going right, with phase change)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify State Points**

| State | Condition | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sat. vapour at −15 °C | 389.2 | 1.7395 |
| 2 | Superheated at 30 °C, s = s₁ | 425.6 | 1.7395 |
| 3 | Sat. liquid at 30 °C | 241.5 | — |
| 4 | After throttling (h₄ = h₃) | 241.5 | — |

**Step 2: Refrigeration Effect (RE)**

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 389.2 - 241.5 = 147.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3: Compressor Work (Wc)**

$$
W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 425.6 - 389.2 = 36.4 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4: COP**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{147.7}{36.4} = 4.058
$$

**Step 5: Final Answer**

$$
\boxed{\text{COP} = 4.06, \quad RE = 147.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}, \quad W_c = 36.4 \; \text{kJ/kg}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Alternative]**

**B.** (a) With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration (VAR) cycle**. State the function of each component. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare the VCR and VAR refrigeration cycles on **at least six** technical parameters, and discuss **two situations** where a VAR system is preferred over a VCR system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working of VAR Cycle (7 marks)**

The **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration (VAR) cycle** uses heat energy (instead of mechanical work) as the primary input. It employs a **refrigerant-absorbent working pair** (commonly NH₃–H₂O or LiBr–H₂O).

**Schematic Flow (from low to high pressure):**

The refrigerant vapour leaving the evaporator (state 1) is routed to the **absorber**, where it is dissolved in a weak solution (absorbent) to form a **strong (rich) solution**. This strong solution is then pumped (state 2 → 3) by a small liquid pump to the **generator** (boiler), where external heat (Qgen) is supplied. The heat drives off the refrigerant vapour (state 5) from the strong solution, which becomes a **weak (lean) solution** again. The vapour passes to the **condenser** (state 7), where it rejects heat (QH) and condenses to liquid. The liquid then passes through the **expansion valve** (state 8) to the **evaporator**, completing the cycle.

**Functions of Each Component:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Evaporator** | Refrigerant absorbs heat QL from the cold space and vaporises. |
| **Absorber** | Refrigerant vapour is absorbed by the weak solution to form strong solution; heat Qabs is rejected. |
| **Pump** | Pumps strong solution from low pressure to high pressure (small work Wp). |
| **Generator** | External heat Qgen desorbs refrigerant vapour from strong solution. |
| **Condenser** | Refrigerant vapour rejects heat QH and condenses to liquid. |
| **Expansion Valve** | Liquid refrigerant is throttled to low pressure. |

**Part (b) — Comparison and Application Scenarios (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table:**

| S.No. | Parameter | VCR | VAR |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy input | Mechanical (shaft/electrical) work | Heat (thermal) energy |
| 2 | COP | Higher (2 to 7) | Lower (0.3 to 0.7) |
| 3 | Moving parts | Many (compressor) | Very few (only small pump) |
| 4 | Noise | High | Negligible |
| 5 | Maintenance | Frequent | Minimal |
| 6 | Capacity | Small to medium | Medium to very large |
| 7 | Cost of operation | Higher (electricity cost) | Lower (if waste heat is free) |

**Two situations where VAR is preferred:**

1. **Availability of low-grade waste heat:** Industries such as chemical plants, sugar mills, and power plants have abundant low-pressure steam or hot flue gases. This waste heat can directly drive a VAR cycle, avoiding the cost of electricity. VCR would require conversion of heat to electricity first, incurring losses.

2. **Silent operation is essential (e.g., libraries, hospitals, residential buildings):** Since the VAR cycle has no large compressor, it operates almost silently. It is therefore preferred in noise-sensitive environments.

*(Bonus point: VAR systems are also preferred in **remote, off-grid locations** where electricity is unavailable but solar thermal or biogas heat is accessible.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Module 4 VCR & VAR**

- [x] **Refrigeration** = transferring heat from cold to hot body using external work/heat.
- [x] **COP of Refrigerator** = $Q_L / W_{net} = (h_1 - h_4) / (h_2 - h_1)$.
- [x] **COP of Heat Pump** = $Q_H / W_{net} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.
- [x] **Carnot COP (Refrigerator)** = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$.
- [x] **VCR cycle has 4 processes**: Isentropic compression, Isobaric condensation, Isenthalpic throttling, Isobaric evaporation.
- [x] **Throttling is isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$) — never isentropic, never isothermal.
- [x] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** — must be memorized.
- [x] **VAR cycle has 6 components**: Evaporator, Absorber, Pump, Generator, Condenser, Expansion valve.
- [x] **Working pairs**: NH₃–H₂O (industrial, T < 0 °C) and LiBr–H₂O (chillers, T > 0 °C).
- [x] **VCR needs high-grade energy (electricity); VAR uses low-grade heat (steam, solar).**
- [x] **VCR has higher COP than VAR**; VAR is chosen for **silent operation, waste heat utilization, and large capacity**.
- [x] **Refrigerant in NH₃–H₂O is NH₃; absorbent is H₂O** — don't mix them up.
- [x] **P-h diagram for VCR** is **clockwise inside the saturation dome**; the line 1→2 is vertical (isentropic).
- [x] **In a P-h diagram**, area under the evaporator line 4→1 = refrigeration effect; area under the condenser line 2→3 = heat rejected.
- [x] **Energy balance for compressor**: $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ (adiabatic, no KE/PE).
- [x] **State point 1** = saturated vapour (or slightly superheated); **State point 3** = saturated liquid.
- [x] **Pump work in VAR is small** but **never zero** in rigorous analysis.
- [x] **Common mistake**: Writing COP with units — **COP is always dimensionless**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Refrigerants and Their Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Refrigerants and Their Importance # 🧊 Module 4: Types of Refrigerants and Their Importance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is a working fluid that undergoes a phase change (primarily vaporization and condensation) within a refrigeration cycle to absorb heat from a region of low temperature (the cold reservoir / refrigerated space) and reject it to a region of higher temperature (the hot reservoir / environment). In the context of **Process Utilities**, refrigerants are the heart of all mechanical refrigeration systems used in chemical, pharmaceutical, food processing, petrochemical, and HVAC industries. In thermodynamic terms, a refrigerant is a substance that operates in a **vapor-compression**, **absorption**, or **steam-jet** cycle, extracting the **latent heat of vaporization** at low pressure/temperature and releasing it at elevated pressure/temperature. **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** - Evaporator Pressure: typically **1–4 bar** (for high-temperature refrigerants) - Condenser Pressure: typically **8–25 bar** - Evaporator Temperature Range: **−40 °C to +10 °C** (industrial chilling) - Refrigerating Effect (RE): measured in **kJ/kg** or **TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** > **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min ≈ 12600 kJ/hr** (this conversion is heavily tested in KTU ESE) --- ### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blood of the Cooling System" Imagine your body’s circulatory system. **Blood** circulates through your body, picks up heat from your organs and limbs (where temperature is high), travels to the skin surface, and releases that heat to the outside air. It then returns cooler to repeat the cycle. A **refrigerant behaves exactly like blood** inside a refrigeration system: | Body Analogy | Refrigeration System | |---|---| | Blood | Refrigerant (e.g., R-134a, NH₃, CO₂) | | Heart (pumps blood) | Compressor | | Body tissues (give up heat) | **Evaporator** (refrigerant absorbs heat) | | Skin (releases heat to air) | **Condenser** (refrigerant rejects heat) | | Lungs (exchange O₂/CO₂) | **Expansion valve** (drops pressure) | | Warm body → Cool body | Refrigerant: low-pressure vapor → high-pressure liquid → flash gas → low-pressure liquid | The refrigerant **circulates in a closed loop**, alternating between **liquid** and **vapor** states, just as blood alternates between oxygenated and deoxygenated states. --- ### 🎯 Why Refrigerants Matter in Process Utilities > [!IMPORTANT] > In chemical process plants, the **choice of refrigerant dictates the lowest temperature achievable, the energy efficiency (COP), the capital cost, the safety profile, and the environmental footprint** of the entire chilling system. A wrong refrigerant choice can lead to: (1) inability to meet product temperature specs, (2) explosion hazards, (3) violation of Montreal/ Kigali Protocols, and (4) excessive power bills. **Industries that critically depend on refrigerant selection:** - **Petrochemical:** LNG liquefaction (−162 °C), ethylene cracking, demethanizer overhead condensers - **Pharmaceutical:** API crystallization, vaccine cold-chain storage - **Food Processing:** Frozen food (−18 °C to −40 °C), dairy chilling - **Air Separation Units (ASU):** Cryogenic N₂/O₂ production - **Plastics & Rubber:** Chilled water for reactor jackets - **Data Centers / HVAC:** Precision cooling --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Refrigerants Refrigerants are classified by their **chemical family**, **temperature range**, and **ASHRAE numbering system**. ### 🧪 A. Classification by Chemical Composition #### (i) Halocarbons (Chlorofluorocarbons, Hydrocarbons with Halogens) These are the most widely used synthetic refrigerants. **1. Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** - Contain **Chlorine, Fluorine, and Carbon** (no Hydrogen) - Examples: **R-11 (CCl₃F)**, **R-12 (CCl₂F₂)**, R-115 - **Fully halogenated** → highly stable → long atmospheric life - **High ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential) — **banned under Montreal Protocol (1987)** **2. Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs)** - Contain **Hydrogen, Chlorine, Fluorine, Carbon** - Examples: **R-22 (CHClF₂)**, R-123, R-124 - Have some H atoms → degrade partially in troposphere (shorter life than CFCs) - **ODP < 1** (less harmful than CFCs) but still being phased out - R-22: still common in legacy industrial chillers **3. Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** - Contain **Hydrogen, Fluorine, Carbon (no Chlorine)** - Examples: **R-134a (CH₂FCF₃)**, **R-404A**, **R-410A**, R-407C, R-507A - **Zero ODP** (no chlorine to attack ozone) - But **high GWP** (Global Warming Potential) — targeted by **Kigali Amendment (2016)** - Most common in **modern commercial and industrial refrigeration** **4. Hydrofluoroolefins (HFOs) — New Generation** - Contain a **C=C double bond** → shorter atmospheric life - Examples: **R-1234yf (CH₂=CFCF₃)**, R-1234ze - **Zero ODP** and **very low GWP (< 1)** - Future of refrigeration per Kigali Amendment #### (ii) Hydrocarbon Refrigerants (Natural Refrigerants) - **Ammonia (R-717, NH₃)** — most important industrial refrigerant - **Propane (R-290)** - **Isobutane (R-600a)** - **Propylene (R-1270)** - **Ethylene (R-1150)** - **CO₂ (R-744)** — used in transcritical cycles - **Air (R-729)** — used in air-cycle refrigeration (aircraft) #### (iii) Inorganic Refrigerants - **Ammonia (NH₃)**, **Water (H₂O, R-718)**, **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Air** #### (iv) Azeotropic & Zeotropic Blends - **Azeotropic:** behave as a single substance (e.g., R-502 — now phased out) - **Zeotropic:** temperature glide during phase change (e.g., **R-404A, R-407C, R-410A**) --- ### 🌡️ B. Classification by Temperature Range | Class | Evaporator Temp Range | Typical Refrigerants | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **High-Temperature** | +10 °C to 0 °C | R-134a, R-22 | Air conditioning, chilled water | | **Medium-Temperature** | 0 °C to −20 °C | R-22, R-404A, NH₃ | Food preservation, dairy | | **Low-Temperature** | −20 °C to −40 °C | R-404A, R-507, NH₃ | Frozen food, ice cream | | **Cryogenic** | < −40 °C | Ethylene, CO₂, N₂, He | LNG, medical cryogenics | --- ### 🏷️ C. ASHRAE Numbering System (R-Number) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **R-number** follows the rule: **R = (Molecular Weight − 40) for saturated hydrocarbons and halocarbons**, with a lowercase letter suffix indicating isomers. Examples: - **R-12:** MW = 12 + 40 = 52 → CCl₂F₂ (correct!) - **R-22:** MW = 22 + 40 = 62 → CHClF₂ (correct!) - **R-134a:** "a" suffix = isomer variant of R-134 - **R-717:** Inorganic, "7" series → Ammonia - **R-718:** Water (H₂O, MW = 18, but coded differently for inorganics: 7 + 18 = 7-18 → R-718) - **R-744:** CO₂ (7 + 44) --- ## 2.2 Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant ### ⚙️ Thermodynamic Properties 1. **Low boiling point** (at atmospheric pressure) — well below desired evaporator temperature 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** (h_fg) — to carry more heat per kg 3. **Low specific volume of vapor** — small compressor displacement 4. **Low compression ratio** — high volumetric efficiency 5. **Moderate condensing pressure** — avoids thick/heavy compressor shells 6. **High critical temperature** — easier condensation above ambient 7. **Low specific heat of liquid** — to prevent flash gas at expansion valve ### 🔒 Safety Properties 8. **Non-toxic** (or low toxicity) — **ASHRAE Safety Group A1** preferred 9. **Non-flammable** — **ASHRAE Safety Group 1** preferred 10. **Non-corrosive** to metals, seals, lubricants ### 🌍 Environmental Properties 11. **Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** — Montreal Protocol compliance 12. **Low GWP (Global Warming Potential)** — Kigali Amendment compliance 13. **Short atmospheric lifetime** — to limit GHG accumulation 14. **Zero or low TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact)** ### 💰 Economic Properties 15. **Low cost**, **chemically stable**, **compatible with common lubricants (POE, PVE, mineral oil)** 16. **Easy leak detection** (high detectability) --- ## 2.3 Environmental Indices — The "Big Three" > [!NOTE] > **These three indices are TESTED in every KTU ESE for this module. Memorize the definitions.** ### 1. Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) Reference substance: **R-11 (CFC-11) = 1.0** $$\text{ODP} = \frac{\text{Mass of O}_3 \text{ destroyed by 1 kg of refrigerant}}{\text{Mass of O}_3 \text{ destroyed by 1 kg of R-11}}$$ | Refrigerant | ODP | |---|---| | R-11, R-12 (CFCs) | **1.0, 1.0** | | R-22 (HCFC) | **0.055** | | R-134a (HFC) | **0** | | NH₃, CO₂, Propane | **0** | ### 2. Global Warming Potential (GWP) Reference substance: **CO₂ = 1.0** (over 100-year time horizon, IPCC AR5) $$\text{GWP} = \frac{\text{Integrated radiative forcing of 1 kg of gas over 100 yrs}}{\text{Integrated radiative forcing of 1 kg of CO}_2 \text{ over 100 yrs}}$$ | Refrigerant | GWP (100-yr) | |---|---| | CO₂ (R-744) | **1** | | R-1234yf | **< 1** (≈ 0.5) | | R-134a | **1430** | | R-404A | **3922** | | R-410A | **2088** | | R-22 | **1810** | | NH₃ | **0** | ### 3. Atmospheric Lifetime (τ) - CFCs: 50–100+ years (very stable, C–Cl bonds resistant to UV) - HCFCs: 1–20 years - HFCs: 1–270 years (R-134a ≈ 14 yrs; R-23 ≈ 228 yrs) - HFOs: days to weeks (double bond breaks easily) - NH₃: days (reacts in troposphere) --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Definition | Symbol / Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_g - h_f = h_1 - h_4$ **[kJ/kg]** | Heat absorbed per kg in evaporator | | 2 | Compressor Work (W_c) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ **[kJ/kg]** | Actual work input (isentropic) | | 3 | Heat Rejected (Q_cond) | $Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$ **[kJ/kg]** | Heat released at condenser | | 4 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ **[dimensionless]** | Refrigeration efficiency | | 5 | Volumetric Refrigerating Capacity (VRC) | $VRC = \dfrac{RE}{v_1}$ **[kJ/m³]** | Cooling per m³ of suction gas | | 6 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{RE} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{h_1 - h_4}$ **[kg/s]** | Refrigerant circulation rate | | 7 | Ton of Refrigeration | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 210 \text{ kJ/min}$ | Standard cooling unit | | 8 | Compressor Displacement (V̇) | $\dot{V} = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 = \dfrac{Q_{evap} \cdot v_1}{RE}$ **[m³/s]** | Physical compressor size | | 9 | Carnot COP (theoretical max) | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ **[dimensionless]** | Upper bound for any cycle | | 10 | ODP / GWP reference | R-11 (ODP=1), CO₂ (GWP=1) | Environmental impact metric | | 11 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{cond}}{P_{evap}}$ | Compressor stress indicator | | 12 | Energy Efficiency Ratio | $EER = \dfrac{Q_{cooling} \text{ [BTU/hr]}}{W_{input} \text{ [W]}}$ | HVAC industry metric | | 13 | TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact) | $TEWI = GWP \cdot L \cdot n + GWP \cdot m \cdot (1-\alpha) + n \cdot E_{annual} \cdot \beta$ **[kg CO₂-eq]** | Cradle-to-grave climate impact | > [!IMPORTANT] > **State-point convention used universally in KTU:** > - **State 1:** Compressor inlet (saturated vapor, evaporator exit) — $h_1, v_1, P_1, T_1$ > - **State 2:** Compressor outlet (superheated vapor, condenser inlet) — $h_2, P_2$ > - **State 3:** Condenser outlet (saturated liquid) — $h_3$ > - **State 4:** Evaporator inlet (after throttling) — $h_4 = h_3$ (isenthalpic expansion) --- ## 2.5 Selection Strategy (Engineering Decision Tree) In **Process Utilities**, refrigerant selection follows this logic: ``` Step 1 → Required evaporator temperature? (Sets lower T_L) Step 2 → Required cooling capacity? (Sets TR) Step 3 → Check refrigerant boiling point < T_L − 5 °C Step 4 → Is refrigerant toxic/flammable permissible? (Industrial vs occupied space) Step 5 → ODP = 0 required? (Montreal/Kigali compliance) Step 6 → GWP target? (Kigali phasedown schedule) Step 7 → Lubricant compatibility & material compatibility Step 8 → Capital + operating cost (LCC analysis) Step 9 → Safety group classification (ASHRAE A1L, A2L, B2L, etc.) ``` --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Coefficient of Performance (COP) of Vapor Compression Cycle ### Setup Consider the **ideal vapor-compression refrigeration cycle** operating between: - **Evaporator temperature** $T_L$ (K) - **Condenser temperature** $T_H$ (K) The cycle consists of four processes: - **1→2:** Isentropic compression in compressor - **2→3:** Constant-pressure heat rejection in condenser - **3→4:** Isenthalpic throttling in expansion valve - **4→1:** Constant-pressure heat absorption in evaporator ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the Evaporator (control volume)** $$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$$ where $h_1$ = enthalpy of saturated vapor leaving evaporator, $h_4$ = enthalpy of liquid-vapor mixture entering evaporator (after throttling). For unit mass ($\dot{m} = 1$): $$q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$ This is the **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**. **Step 2: Apply the First Law to the Compressor (adiabatic, isentropic)** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$ This is the **compressor work input per kg**. **Step 3: Define COP as the Ratio of Desired Effect to Work Input** $$COP = \frac{\text{Desired Output (cooling)}}{\text{Required Input (work)}} = \frac{q_L}{w_c}$$ $$COP = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ **Step 4: Derive the Carnot (Theoretical Maximum) COP** For a **reversible (Carnot) refrigeration cycle** operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$: $$\frac{q_L}{q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} \quad \text{(from Carnot cycle entropy balance)}$$ and $w_c = q_H - q_L$, so: $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{q_L}{q_H - q_L} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ Substituting the absolute temperatures (in Kelvin): $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Step 5: Establish the Second-Law Bound (Efficiency Statement)** Since the actual cycle is irreversible, the real COP must satisfy: $$COP_{actual} \leq COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** When the desired temperature $T_L$ is very low (cryogenic applications), the denominator $T_H - T_L$ becomes large, and COP drops sharply. For example, at $T_L = 233$ K (−40 °C) and $T_H = 308$ K (35 °C), $COP_{Carnot} = 233/75 ≈ 3.1$, meaning even a perfect cycle needs ~1 kW of work to produce 3.1 kW of cooling. Real-world COP is typically 40–60% of Carnot. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate and Compressor Displacement ### Given - Required cooling load: $Q_e$ in **kW** (or **TR**) - Refrigerating effect: $RE = h_1 - h_4$ in **kJ/kg** - Specific volume at compressor inlet: $v_1$ in **m³/kg** ### Derivation **Step 1: Energy balance on evaporator** $$Q_e = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4) = \dot{m} \cdot RE$$ Solving for mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} \quad \text{(kg/s, if Q in kW = kJ/s)}$$ **Step 2: Volumetric flow rate at compressor suction** $$\dot{V}_1 = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 = \frac{Q_e \cdot v_1}{RE} \quad \text{(m³/s)}$$ **Step 3: Compressor displacement (including volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$)** $$\dot{V}_{disp} = \frac{\dot{V}_1}{\eta_v} = \frac{Q_e \cdot v_1}{\eta_v \cdot RE}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU ESE-style):** A refrigeration system uses R-134a to provide 10 TR of cooling. The compressor inlet conditions are $T_1 = -10$ °C (saturated vapor). For R-134a at $-10$ °C: - $h_1 = 392.3$ kJ/kg (saturated vapor) - $h_4 = h_3$ at 35 °C = 249.7 kJ/kg (saturated liquid at condenser pressure) - $v_1 = 0.0993$ m³/kg Compute: RE, $\dot{m}$, $\dot{V}_{disp}$ (assume $\eta_v = 0.85$). **Step A: Compute Refrigerating Effect** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392.3 - 249.7 = 142.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step B: Convert 10 TR to kW** $$Q_e = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17 \text{ kW}$$ **Step C: Compute Mass Flow Rate** $$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} = \frac{35.17}{142.6} = 0.2467 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step D: Compute Compressor Displacement** $$\dot{V}_{disp} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_v} = \frac{0.2467 \times 0.0993}{0.85} = 0.0288 \text{ m³/s} \approx 1.73 \text{ m³/min}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Refrigerants | Property | R-12 | R-22 | R-134a | R-404A | NH₃ (R-717) | CO₂ (R-744) | R-1234yf | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Boiling Point (°C, 1 atm) | −29.8 | −40.8 | −26.1 | −46.5 | −33.3 | −78.4 (sublimes) | −29.4 | | Critical Temp (°C) | 112 | 96 | 101 | 72 | 133 | 31 | 95 | | Critical Pressure (bar) | 41.4 | 49.9 | 40.6 | 37.3 | 113 | 73.8 | 34 | | Latent Heat h_fg (kJ/kg) @ 0 °C | 152 | 205 | 198 | ~170 | 1377 | 232 (at −40 °C) | 180 | | ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | GWP (100-yr) | 10900 | 1810 | 1430 | 3922 | 0 | 1 | < 1 | | ASHRAE Safety Group | A1 | A1 | A1 | A1 | B2L | A1 | A2L | | Toxicity | Low | Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Low | | Flammability | None | None | None | None | Mild | None | Mild | | Lubricant | Mineral | Mineral | POE | POE | Mineral (dry) | POE | POE/PVE | | Typical Application | Legacy (banned) | Legacy AC | Modern AC, chillers | Commercial freezers | Industrial cold storage, process cooling | Transcritical, hot climates | Automotive AC, future HFC replacement | --- ## 3.4 Detailed Comparison — Ammonia vs HFCs ### 🟢 Why Ammonia (R-717) Remains the King of Industrial Refrigeration > [!NOTE] > **NH₃ has the highest latent heat of vaporization of ANY practical refrigerant — 1377 kJ/kg at 0 °C** (vs ~200 kJ/kg for HFCs). This is **6–7× higher** than R-134a. **Consequence:** For the same cooling load, the **mass flow rate of NH₃ is ~7× lower** than R-134a. Smaller pipes, smaller valves, smaller compressor. **Example Comparison for 100 TR cooling load:** | Refrigerant | RE (kJ/kg) | $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) | $\dot{V}$ (m³/min) | Pipe Size (rel.) | |---|---|---|---|---| | R-134a | 142.6 | 2.47 | 14.7 | Large | | NH₃ | 1106 (at −10 °C) | 0.318 | 0.46 | **Small** | **Drawbacks of Ammonia:** - **Toxic** (Odor threshold: 5 ppm; IDLH: 300 ppm) — strict OSHA regulations - **Mildly flammable** (LFL = 15%, UFL = 28%) — but hard to ignite - **Reacts with copper** — copper/brass piping NOT allowed (use steel) - **Higher discharge temperatures** — risk of oil breakdown in compressor **Why HFCs Dominate Commercial/AC:** - Non-toxic, non-flammable, safe in occupied spaces - Drop-in replacement for phased-out CFCs - Compatible with existing metallurgy - But: **high GWP** (R-134a = 1430, R-404A = 3922) **Why CO₂ is Back (R-744):** - Zero ODP, GWP = 1, non-toxic, non-flammable - Excellent for **transcritical cycles** in hot climates - Challenges: very high pressures (≥ 90–120 bar), low critical temp - Used in: supermarket refrigeration, heat pump water heaters, automotive (BMW, Mercedes) --- # 4.
