# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]
**"Define Schedule Number of a pipe. How is it related to design pressure and allowable stress?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **Schedule Number (Sch No)** of a pipe is a dimensionless number introduced by the American Standards Association (ASA) in 1939 to standardize wall thickness for a given pipe size, independent of the material grade.

It is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{Schedule Number} = \dfrac{1000 \times P}{S}$$

where $P$ = internal design pressure (psi) and $S$ = allowable fiber stress of the material at the design temperature (psi).

**Significance**:
- Higher the design pressure $P$ (for a fixed $S$), higher the Schedule Number, hence thicker the wall.
- Higher the allowable stress $S$ (e.g., alloy steel vs. carbon steel), lower the Schedule Number for the same pressure, allowing thinner walls.
- The Schedule Number maps to discrete standard wall thicknesses (Sch 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160, XXS) catalogued in ASME B36.10M.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**"Differentiate between Nominal Pipe Size (NPS) and Diameter Nominal (DN). Why are two systems in concurrent use globally?"**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **NPS (Nominal Pipe Size)** | **DN (Diamètre Nominal / Diameter Nominal)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | North American (ASME/ANSI) | European (ISO 6708) |
| **Unit** | Dimensionless (inches-based) | Dimensionless (millimetre-based) |
| **Examples** | NPS 1/2, NPS 1, NPS 2, NPS 4, NPS 6, NPS 24 | DN 15, DN 25, DN 50, DN 100, DN 150, DN 600 |
| **Relationship** | NPS 1" = DN 25; NPS 2" = DN 50; NPS 6" = DN 150 | DN 50 = NPS 2" (approximate, not exact OD) |
| **Designation basis** | Historical ID (for NPS ≤ 12); actual OD (for NPS ≥ 14) | Generic diameter, not equal to actual OD |
| **Standards** | ASME B36.10M, B36.19M, ASME B16.5 | ISO 4200, ISO 1127, EN 10255, EN 10217 |

**Why two systems**: Globalization of engineering procurement. North American and European EPC contractors use their indigenous systems; a "1" mapping (e.g., NPS 1 ↔ DN 25) provides a common lexicon for project specifications, allowing bidders from either region to quote.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern (ESE Module):** Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks, allowing internal choice between two questions A and B (you answer either A or B).

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Classify pipes used in process industries based on **(i) material of construction** and **(ii) service application**, giving two examples in each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **ASME B31 series of piping codes** with reference to their scope and application areas. Which code is most commonly followed in chemical and petrochemical plants, and why? **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution A(a) — Classification of Pipes

**(i) Classification by Material of Construction**

Pipes are classified under three broad families:

**1. Ferrous (Iron-based) Pipes**
- **Carbon Steel (CS)** pipes — ASTM A53 (Gr. A/B), ASTM A106 (Gr. A/B/C) — for general process, steam, and water service up to 425 °C.
  - *Example*: Sch 40 A106 Gr. B seamless pipe for refinery hydrocarbon service.
- **Alloy Steel** pipes — ASTM A335 (P1, P11, P22, P91) — for high-temperature (up to 600 °C) creep-resistant service.
  - *Example*: 1½ Cr-½ Mo (P11) pipe for ethylene-cracker hot oil circuit.
- **Stainless Steel (SS)** pipes — ASTM A312 (304, 304L, 316, 316L, 321, 347) — for corrosive and hygienic service.
  - *Example*: SS 316L EP pipe for pharmaceutical water-for-injection (WFI) lines.
- **Cast Iron (CI) and Ductile Iron (DI)** — for low-pressure buried water and sewage lines.

**2. Non-Ferrous Metal Pipes**
- **Copper-Nickel (90/10, 70/30)** — for seawater cooling, shipboard, and desalination.
  - *Example*: Cu-Ni 90/10 NPS 12 Sch 40 for coastal power plant cooling.
- **Titanium Gr. 2** — for wet chlorine, hot oxidizing acids, deep-sea.
- **Nickel Alloys (Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy)** — for severe chemicals like HCl, HF.
  - *Example*: Hastelloy C-276 for hydrochloric acid transfer.

**3. Non-Metallic Pipes**
- **Thermoplastics** — PVC, CPVC, HDPE, PP, PVDF — for low-pressure chemical, water, gas.
  - *Example*: HDPE PE-100 pipe for city gas distribution (SDR-11).
- **Fibre-Reinforced Plastic (FRP/GRP)** — for highly corrosive and large-diameter lines.
  - *Example*: GRP pipe for chlor-alkali brine.
- **Lined Pipes** — Carbon steel with internal PTFE/PVDF/rubber lining — combines strength and corrosion resistance.
  - *Example*: PTFE-lined CS pipe for concentrated H₂SO₄.

**(ii) Classification by Service Application**

| Service Type | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Piping** | Carries raw materials, intermediates, products within the process boundary | Reactor feed line, product rundown line |
| **Utility Piping** | Services like steam, cooling water, instrument air, nitrogen, fuel | Boiler steam line, cooling water return |
| **Power Piping** | High-pressure/temperature steam, boiler feed water in power plants | Main steam line (HP/LP) |
| **Pipeline (Cross-Country)** | Long-distance hydrocarbon transport | Crude oil trunk line, gas transmission |
| **Hygienic / Sanitary** | Pharma, food, beverage, semiconductor, WFI | WFI loop, purified water |

### ✅ Model Solution A(b) — ASME B31 Series Codes

The **ASME B31 Code for Pressure Piping** is a family of standards, each addressing a specific industrial sector:

| Code | Title | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **B31.1** | Power Piping | Electric-power generating stations, industrial and institutional steam plants, heating, and district heating systems |
| **B31.2** | *(obsolete; replaced by B31.3)* | Fuel Gas Piping (retired 2010) |
| **B31.3** | Process Piping | Refineries, chemical, pharmaceutical, textile, paper, semiconductor, cryogenic, and related process plants |
| **B31.4** | Pipeline Transportation Systems for Liquid Hydrocarbons and Other Liquids | Crude oil, refined products, CO₂ pipelines |
| **B31.5** | Refrigeration Piping and Heat Transfer Components | HVAC&R, ammonia and halogenated refrigerant systems |
| **B31.7** | *(obsolete; integrated into B31.3)* | Nuclear power piping |
| **B31.8** | Gas Transmission and Distribution Piping Systems | Natural gas transmission, distribution mains, service lines |
| **B31.9** | Building Services Piping | Hot- and cold-water, sanitary, storm-drain, and gas in multi-storey buildings |
| **B31.11** | Slurry Transportation Piping Systems | Concentrated ore, mineral, coal slurries |
| **B31.12** | Hydrogen Piping and Pipelines | Hydrogen production, storage, transport (green hydrogen economy) |

**Most commonly used code in chemical/petrochemical plants → ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)**.

**Reasons for B31.3's dominance**:
1. **Comprehensive fluid classification** — Normal Fluid Service, Category D (non-toxic, non-flammable), Category M (highly toxic), and High Pressure — each with progressively stringent requirements.
2. **Material breadth** — carbon steel, alloy, SS, non-ferrous, non-metallic, lined — all covered.
3. **Detailed design rules** — internal/external pressure design, flexibility analysis, support spacing, corrosion allowance, and welding procedure specification (WPS) and qualification (WPQ) requirements.
4. **Mandatory testing and inspection** — hydrostatic test, pneumatic test, radiographic (RT) and ultrasonic (UT) NDE requirements.
5. **Wide global acceptance** — adopted by IS, EN, and most national regulators (CWC India, OSHA USA, HSE UK).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **Schedule Number system** of pipe classification. Derive the relationship between Schedule Number, design pressure, and allowable stress. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe of nominal size **NPS 6" (DN 150)** carrying water is required to handle a flow of **250 m³/h** at a design pressure of **15 bar (g)** and design temperature of **80 °C**. The material selected is **ASTM A106 Gr. B** with allowable stress 138 MPa. Determine the **Schedule Number** and check whether **Sch 40** is adequate. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution B(a) — Schedule Number System

**Definition**: The Schedule Number is a dimensionless, integer-based wall-thickness designation defined in **ASME B36.10M** (for carbon/alloy steel) and **ASME B36.19M** (for stainless steel). For a given NPS, the higher the Schedule Number, the thicker the wall.

**Historical Origin**: Introduced in 1939 by the American Standards Association, derived from the **Birmingham Wire Gauge (BWG)** thickness-pressure-stress relationship.

**Derivation**:

Step 1: For a thin-walled cylindrical pipe under internal pressure, the hoop (membrane) stress is:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t} \leq \dfrac{S}{E}$$

Step 2: For a seamless pipe (joint efficiency $E = 1$) and neglecting corrosion allowance, the required wall thickness for design is:

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S}$$

Step 3: Define the **Schedule Number** as the ratio of design pressure to allowable stress, scaled by 1000:

$$\text{Schedule Number} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{S}$$

Step 4: Substituting this into the wall-thickness formula (using $D_o$ in inches):

$$t_{\text{inch}} = \dfrac{D_{o,\text{inch}}}{2000} \cdot \text{Sch No}$$

Step 5: The modern ASME B36.10M uses the corrected equation that matches the standard tabulated wall thickness values:

$$t_{\text{inch}} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot D_{o,\text{inch}} \cdot \text{Sch No}}{1000 + 0.5 \cdot \text{Sch No}}$$

Step 6: For NPS 4" (OD = 4.500"), Sch 40 → $t = \dfrac{0.5 \times 4.5 \times 40}{1000 + 20} = \dfrac{90}{1020} = 0.0882$ in (BWG). The actual ASME value is **0.237 in**, which is the historical "Standard (STD)" wall carried forward and labelled "Sch 40" for convenience.

**Sketch (Tabular Representation)**:

```
 NPS (in) | OD (in) | Sch 10 (t) | Sch 40 STD (t) | Sch 80 XS (t) | Sch 160 (t) | XXS (t)
   1/2    |  0.840  |   0.083    |    0.109       |    0.147      |   0.187     |  0.294
   1      |  1.315  |   0.109    |    0.133       |    0.179      |   0.250     |  0.358
   2      |  2.375  |   0.065    |    0.154       |    0.218      |   0.344     |  0.436
   4      |  4.500  |   0.120    |    0.237       |    0.337      |   0.531     |  0.674
   6      |  6.625  |   0.134    |    0.280       |    0.432      |   0.719     |  0.864
  10      | 10.750  |   0.165    |    0.365       |    0.500      |   0.844     |  1.000
```

**Designation Standards**:
- **STD** = Standard (Sch 40 for most NPS).
- **XS** = Extra Strong (Sch 80 for most NPS).
- **XXS** = Double Extra Strong (heaviest standard).
- Suffix "S" (e.g., 10S, 40S) = Stainless steel per ASME B36.19M.

### ✅ Model Solution B(b) — Worked Numerical

**Given**:
- NPS 6" → $D_o = 168.3$ mm = 6.625 in
- Material: ASTM A106 Gr. B, $S = 138$ MPa = 20,015 psi (≈ 138 × 145.04 = 20,015 psi)
- Design pressure: $P = 15$ bar(g) ≈ $15 \times 14.504 = 217.5$ psi
- Joint efficiency $E = 1$ (seamless pipe, conservative assumption)

**Step 1: Compute the Schedule Number from $1000 P / S$**:

$$\text{Sch} = \dfrac{1000 \times 217.5}{20015} = \dfrac{217500}{20015} = 10.87$$

**Step 2: Round up to the next standard Schedule Number**:

The next standard value ≥ 10.87 is **Sch 20** (Sch 10 is 10 < 10.87, so insufficient).

Thus, the **calculated minimum Schedule Number is Sch 20**.

**Step 3: Check the Sch 40 adequacy** (as a safety margin, Sch 40 is the next standard heavy grade):

- For NPS 6" Sch 40: $t = 7.11$ mm = 0.280 in (from ASME B36.10M).
- Required wall thickness (recomputed from the exact thin-wall formula, neglecting $c$ and $E = 1$):
$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1683}{2 \times 138 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{252450}{276 \times 10^{6}} = 9.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.914 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply a safety/quality factor and corrosion allowance**:

For moderate service, ASME B31.3 recommends:
- Corrosion allowance $c = 1.5$ mm (for carbon steel water service).
- Design thickness $t_{\text{design}} = t + c = 0.914 + 1.5 = 2.414$ mm.
- Adding a 2× safety factor on the calculated thin-wall (or following the design-factor method): $t_{\text{design}} \approx 3$ mm minimum.

**Step 5: Compare with available standard schedules**:

| Schedule | Wall Thickness (mm) | Adequate? |
|---|---|---|
| **Sch 10** | 2.77 | NO (marginally less than 3 mm) |
| **Sch 20** | 3.91 | YES — meets minimum 3 mm |
| **Sch 40 (STD)** | 7.11 | YES — conservative, recommended for water at 15 bar |
| **Sch 80 (XS)** | 10.97 | Over-specified, uneconomical |

**Step 6: Conclusion**:

The calculated minimum schedule is **Sch 20** based on $1000 P / S$ alone. Considering the standard engineering practice of adding a corrosion allowance of 1.5 mm and adopting the next standard schedule above the calculated value:

➡ **Sch 40 (STD) is adequate and recommended** for this water service at 15 bar(g) and 80 °C using ASTM A106 Gr. B seamless pipe.

**Final Pipe Class**: **NPS 6" (DN 150), Sch 40, ASTM A106 Gr. B, Seamless, Butt-Welded**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — Marks are heavily lost when students mix psi with MPa, or use $D_o$ in mm with the 1000 P/S formula which expects inches. Convert everything **consistently (either all SI or all FPS)** before substituting.
> 2. **Corrosion allowance** is often forgotten — the design wall thickness must include it, especially for carbon-steel water services.
> 3. **Joint efficiency $E$** — for ERW pipes use $E = 0.85$, for EFW use $E = 0.80$. Examiners test this in follow-up sub-questions.
> 4. **Rounding direction** — always round **up** the Schedule Number, never down (a thinner-than-required pipe fails in service).
> 5. **Sch 10S ≠ Sch 10** — the "S" suffix is for stainless steel per B36.19M and has different (thinner) wall values than B36.10M. Many students confuse these.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Core Concepts (10 Rapid Bullets)**

- ⏺ **Pipe = tubing** for fluid conveyance; **Piping = complete system** including fittings, flanges, valves, supports, and insulation.
- ⏺ **NPS** (inch-based) and **DN** (mm-based) are the two sizing systems; NPS 1" ↔ DN 25, NPS 2" ↔ DN 50, NPS 6" ↔ DN 150.
- ⏺ **Schedule Number = 1000 P / S** — higher $P$ or lower $S$ ⇒ higher schedule ⇒ thicker wall.
- ⏺ **Sch 40 = STD**, **Sch 80 = XS**, **Sch 160 = XXS** (general equivalences for small/medium NPS).
- ⏺ **"S" suffix** (e.g., 10S, 40S) denotes **stainless steel** schedule per ASME B36.19M.
- ⏺ **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)** is the most-used piping code in chemical/petrochemical plants.
- ⏺ **B31.1** = power, **B31.4** = liquid hydrocarbon pipelines, **B31.5** = refrigeration, **B31.8** = gas, **B31.9** = buildings, **B31.12** = hydrogen.
- ⏺ **ASTM A53** = general service CS, **A106** = high-temp CS, **A312** = SS, **A335** = alloy, **API 5L** = line pipe.
- ⏺ **Velocity-based sizing** — liquids 1–3 m/s, steam 25–40 m/s, gas 15–30 m/s. Always **round up** the calculated NPS.
- ⏺ **Pressure drop** uses the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** with friction factor from the **Moody chart** or **Colebrook–White** equation; include equivalent length for fittings.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Final-Word Mnemonics**
>
> **"M-S-C"** for pipe specification: **Material + Schedule + Connection**.
>
> **"S-M-E-P-D"** for code priority: **Service → Material → Environment → Pressure → Design Code (ASME B31.x)**.
>
> **"P-V-ρ"** for sizing: **Pick Velocity first, then Volume flow, then density to find Diameter**.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid**
> 1. Confusing NPS with actual diameter (NPS 6" has OD 6.625", not 6"!).
> 2. Forgetting to include fittings' equivalent length in pressure-drop calculation.
> 3. Using carbon-steel pipe (Sch 40) for concentrated HCl — will fail in days.
> 4. Specifying Sch 10S (stainless) data for Sch 10 (carbon steel) — different tables, different walls.
> 5. Ignoring the corrosion allowance in the Barlow wall-thickness design.
> 6. Citing obsolete standards (e.g., B31.2, B31.7) — these have been retired and merged into B31.3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piping Components: Pipes, Fittings, Flanges, Gaskets, and Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT611 - PROCESS UTILITIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piping Components: Pipes, Fittings, Flanges, Gaskets, and Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Process Piping System?

A **process piping system** is an integrated network of pipes, fittings, flanges, gaskets, valves, and supporting elements (hangers, supports, expansion joints, instruments) designed to convey fluids (liquids, gases, vapours, slurries, or two-phase mixtures) safely and economically between process units within a chemical plant, refinery, or utility block. The design, material selection, and component selection are governed by codes such as **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)**, **B31.1 (Power Piping)**, and **IS 2825 / IS 1239** for Indian installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A piping system is the *arterial backbone* of any process plant. While the *vessels* hold the chemicals, the *pipes* move them. A single failure in a piping component (e.g., a ruptured flange gasket) can lead to catastrophic loss of containment, fire, toxic release, and huge economic loss.

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Process Piping (Module-4 Scope)

| # | Component | Functional Role | Intuition (Real-World Analogy) |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Pipes** | Conduit — defines the flow path and diameter | Like the *arteries* in a human body that carry blood |
| 2 | **Fittings** | Geometry changer — alters direction, branches, or reduces size | Like *road junctions, curves, and roundabouts* |
| 3 | **Flanges** | Detachable joint — allows connection, maintenance, and isolation | Like a *screw-cap on a bottle* or a *detachable pipe union* |
| 4 | **Gaskets** | Static seal — fills the micro-gap between two flange faces to prevent leakage | Like the *rubber ring inside a pressure cooker lid* |
| 5 | **Valves** | Flow controller — starts, stops, throttles, or prevents reverse flow | Like the *tap* in your kitchen sink or a *traffic signal* |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Water Supply

Think of a large apartment building:
- **Pipes** = the vertical risers and horizontal lines that carry water from the overhead tank.
- **Fittings** = the elbows at corners, T-junctions where branches meet, and reducers where pipe size changes from large to small.
- **Flanges** = the bolted flanges on the pump discharge or at each floor's connection point — they can be *unbolted* for cleaning.
- **Gaskets** = the thin rubber/cloth ring pressed between two flanges that *seals* the joint and prevents drips.
- **Valves** = the *gate valves* on each floor, the *ball valve* near the tank, and the *check valve* on the pump that prevents backflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A piping system is only as strong as its **weakest component**. KTU exam questions frequently test the *interdependence* of these five elements — e.g., "How does the choice of flange affect the choice of gasket and the bolt torque required?"

## 1.4 Governing Standards at a Glance

- **ASME B31.3** — Process Piping (most common for chemical plants)
- **ASME B16.5** — Pipe Flanges and Flanged Fittings (NPS ½ to 24)
- **ASME B16.9** — Factory-Made Wrought Butt-Welding Fittings
- **ASME B16.34** — Valves — Flanged, Threaded, and Welding End
- **MSS-SP-25** — Standard Marking System for Valves, Fittings, Flanges
- **IS 2062 / ASTM A106 / A312** — Common pipe material standards
- **API 600 / API 602 / API 6D** — Valve design and testing standards

## 1.5 Why this Module Matters for KTU Examiners

The Module 4 content is *high-weightage* because it tests:
1. The student's **ability to size and select** pipes, fittings, and valves for a given duty.
2. The student's **understanding of pressure–temperature ratings** and material compatibility.
3. The student's **awareness of safety**, because piping failures are the single largest cause of loss-of-containment incidents in the process industry (per CCPS / OSHA data).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PIPES — The Flow Conduit

### 2.1.1 Pipe Sizing — How is Pipe Size Specified?

Pipes are specified by **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** in inches (or **DN** in mm). NPS is *not* the actual inside diameter — it is a dimensionless designator. The actual dimensions (ID, OD, wall thickness) are given in **ASME B36.10M** (carbon/alloy steel) and **B36.19M** (stainless steel).

> [!NOTE]
> For NPS 1/8 to NPS 12, the OD is fixed and wall thickness varies with **Schedule Number**. For NPS ≥ 14, OD equals NPS (inches).

### 2.1.2 Schedule Number

The **Schedule Number (Sch)** is a dimensionless number that expresses the *ratio of design pressure to allowable stress* for a given pipe size.

$$
\text{Schedule Number} = \frac{P \times 1000}{S}
$$

where:
- $P$ = internal design pressure (psi)
- $S$ = allowable stress at design temperature (psi)

Common schedules used in industry: **Sch 5, 10, 20, 40 (STD), 40S, 60, 80 (XS), 80S, 100, 120, 140, 160, XXS**.

### 2.1.3 Pipe Wall Thickness — ASME B31.3 Straight Pipe Formula

The *required* minimum wall thickness for a straight pipe under internal pressure (per ASME B31.3, para. 304.1.2) is:

$$
t_m = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \left(S \cdot E + P \cdot Y\right)} + C
$$

where:
- $t_m$ = minimum required thickness (mm or in)
- $P$ = internal design pressure (MPa or psi)
- $D$ = outside diameter of pipe (mm or in)
- $S$ = allowable stress at design temperature (MPa or psi)
- $E$ = quality factor (longitudinal joint factor, 0.60 to 1.00; for seamless pipe $E = 1.00$)
- $Y$ = coefficient from Table 304.1.1 (0.4 for ferritic steels < 482 °C; 0.0 for austenitic steels)
- $C$ = corrosion + mechanical allowances (typically **1.5 mm to 3.0 mm**)

The **purchased (nominal) wall thickness** must always be greater than $t_m$ after subtracting mill tolerance (typically 12.5 % negative).

### 2.1.4 Common Pipe Materials (with applications)

| Material | ASTM / IS Grade | Typical Application |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Carbon Steel | A106 Gr.B, A53, IS 1239 | Steam, water, air, non-corrosive hydrocarbons |
| Low-Temp CS | A333 Gr.6 | LPG, ethylene, propylene (T < –29 °C) |
| Stainless Steel 304 / 304L | A312 TP304/304L | Food, pharma, mild corrosives |
| Stainless Steel 316 / 316L | A312 TP316/316L | Chlorides, seawater, acetic acid |
| Alloy 825 / C-276 | B423 / B575 | H₂SO₄, HCl, hot caustic |
| HDPE / PP / PVDF | — | Acid drainage, demin water |

## 2.2 FITTINGS — Changing Direction, Branching, Sizing

### 2.2.1 Functional Classification

| Function | Fitting | Symbol in P&ID |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| 90° direction change | **Elbow** (Long Radius LR / Short Radius SR) | ┘ or └ |
| 45° direction change | **45° Elbow** | ∠ |
| Branching | **Tee** (Equal / Reducing) | ⊥ |
| Branching at 45° | **Lateral** (Y-type) | ∢ |
| Size reduction | **Reducer** (Concentric / Eccentric) | ▷ or ⊲ |
| Pipe end closure | **Cap** | ─█ |
| Permanent branch | **Weldolet / Sockolet / Nipolet** | branch symbol |
| Inline cleaning | **Strainer / Y-strainer** | ⊳⊳ |

### 2.2.2 Pressure Drop Across Fittings (Equivalent Length Method)

Fittings cause *additional* frictional loss. They are accounted for by an **equivalent straight length (Lₑ)** of pipe of the same nominal size:

$$
\Delta P_{fitting} = \frac{f \cdot L_e}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

Typical $L_e/D$ values (per Crane TP-410, widely used in KTU problem sets):

| Fitting | $L_e / D$ |
|---------|----------|
| 90° Elbow, LR | 30 |
| 90° Elbow, SR | 16 (sometimes used LR × 0.6) |
| 45° Elbow | 16 |
| Tee, line flow | 20 |
| Tee, branch flow | 60 |
| Gate valve, fully open | 8 |
| Globe valve, fully open | 340 |
| Check valve, swing | 100 |
| Ball valve, fully open | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a *quick* estimate, a $90°$ LR elbow is equivalent to ~30 pipe diameters of straight pipe. A globe valve is the **worst offender** (~340 D), which is why it should be avoided in long service lines.

## 2.3 FLANGES — Detachable Joints

### 2.3.1 Flange Types (ASME B16.5)

| Type | Design | Application |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Weld Neck (WN)** | Long tapered hub, butt-welded to pipe | High pressure, high temperature, cyclic duty, severe service |
| **Slip-On (SO)** | Pipe slips over flange, fillet welded | Low pressure, non-critical, low cost |
| **Socket Weld (SW)** | Pipe socketed into flange, fillet welded | Small bore (≤ NPS 2), high pressure |
| **Threaded (THD)** | Female threads on flange bore | Low pressure, non-flammable, utility water/air |
| **Lap Joint (LJ)** | Flange rotates freely over stub-end | Frequent bolt-up changes, stainless systems |
| **Blind (BL)** | Solid disk, no bore | Test headers, vessel closures, future connections |

### 2.3.2 Flange Pressure–Temperature Ratings

Flanges are classified into **pressure classes**: **150, 300, 400, 600, 900, 1500, 2500 lb** (in ANSI) and **PN 6, 10, 16, 25, 40, 63, 100** (in ISO). The *rating* defines the maximum allowable non-shock gauge pressure at a given temperature, published in ASME B16.5 Table 2-1.1 to 2-3.11.

> [!NOTE]
> **The higher the temperature, the lower the rating.** For example, an A216-WCB gate valve with Class 300 flanges is rated ~**7.96 MPa at 38 °C** but only ~**5.10 MPa at 425 °C**.

### 2.3.3 Flange Face Types (FF, RF, RTJ)

- **Flat Face (FF)** — Cast iron, low pressure; mates with full-face gasket.
- **Raised Face (RF)** — Most common; 2 mm (small bore) or 1.6 mm raised surface, mates with ring gasket.
- **Ring-Type Joint (RTJ)** — Grooved face for metal ring gasket; high pressure, high temperature (e.g., refinery hydrocracker service).

## 2.4 GASKETS — Static Sealing Elements

### 2.4.1 Gasket Selection Criteria

1. **Temperature** of service.
2. **Pressure** of service.
3. **Chemical compatibility** with the fluid.
4. **Flange face** finish (FF, RF, RTJ).
5. **Cost** and **availability**.

### 2.4.2 Gasket Types (with applications)

| Gasket | Material | Service |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **Compressed Asbestos Fibre (CAF)** | Asbestos + binder | Obsolete; legacy plants only |
| **Non-Asbestos Fibre (NAF)** | Aramid / graphite / NBR | General water, steam, air |
| **Spiral-Wound (SW)** | Graphite + SS windings + inner/outer rings | Refinery, high T, high P, cyclic |
| **Ring-Joint (RTJ)** | Soft iron, SS 304/316, Monel | Very high P, high T (≥ Class 600) |
| **Kammprofile** | Graphite + SS core | Heat exchangers, reformer service |
| **PTFE (expanded)** | Virgin / filled PTFE | Strong acids, pharma, food |
| **Rubber (NBR / EPDM / Viton / Neoprene)** | Elastomer | Water, mild chemicals, low pressure |
| **Metal-jacketed** | SS 316 jacket + filler | Shell-and-tube exchangers, low P |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Spiral-wound gaskets are the **industry workhorse** for refinery and petrochemical service from Class 150 to Class 2500. They combine the resilience of graphite with the strength of stainless steel windings and tolerate both temperature and pressure cycling.

### 2.4.3 Gasket Stress and Bolt Load (simplified KTU formula)

The required bolt load to seal a gasket is:

$$
W_{m2} = \pi \cdot b \cdot G \cdot y
$$

where:
- $W_{m2}$ = minimum bolt load for seating (operating condition)
- $b$ = effective gasket width
- $G$ = gasket mean diameter
- $y$ = gasket seating stress (from ASME B16.5 Appendix)

## 2.5 VALVES — Flow Control Devices

### 2.5.1 Valve Classification by Function

| Function | Typical Valves |
|----------|----------------|
| **Isolation (on/off)** | Gate, Ball, Plug, Butterfly |
| **Throttling** | Globe, Needle, Control valve |
| **Non-return (check)** | Swing check, Lift check, Dual-plate check, Tilting-disc |
| **Pressure relief** | Safety valve, Relief valve, Rupture disc |
| **Special** | Diaphragm (sterile service), Pinch (slurry), Cryogenic |

### 2.5.2 Valve Flow Coefficient $C_v$

The **flow coefficient $C_v$** is the *most asked* valve-sizing parameter in KTU papers. It is defined as the *gallons per minute of water at 60 °F* that will pass through the valve with a **1 psi pressure drop**.

For **liquids (non-choked)**:

$$
C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}}
$$

For **gases and vapours** (valid for $y < 0.5$, where $y = \Delta P / P_1$):

$$
C_v = \frac{Q}{N_6} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{SG \cdot T}{x \cdot P_1^2}}
$$

where $N_6$ is a numerical constant (≈ 732 for SI), $x = \Delta P / P_1$, $T$ = absolute temperature.

### 2.5.3 Comparison Table — Valves at a Glance (KTU favourite)

| Valve | ΔP (fully open) | Leakage Class | Throttling | Cost | Best For |
|-------|-----------------|---------------|------------|------|----------|
| **Gate** | Very low | Class IV–V | Poor | Medium | Isolation, full bore flow |
| **Globe** | High (≈ 8–10× gate) | Class IV–V | Excellent | Medium | Throttling, tight shutoff |
| **Ball** | Very low | Class VI | Good | Low | Quick isolation, low cost |
| **Butterfly** | Low | Class VI | Good | Low | Large bore, low pressure, water/air |
| **Plug** | Low | Class IV | Good | Medium | Slurries, high T (with sleeve) |
| **Check (swing)** | Moderate | — (non-return) | N/A | Low | Pump discharge, line protection |
| **Diaphragm** | Moderate | Bubble-tight | Good | High | Pharma, sterile, corrosive |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | $t_m = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)} + C$ | Min pipe wall thickness | Pipe sizing (B31.3) |
| 2 | $\text{Sch} = \dfrac{P \times 1000}{S}$ | Schedule number | Pipe selection |
| 3 | $L_e = K \cdot D / f$ | Equivalent length of fitting | Pressure drop across fittings |
| 4 | $h_L = K \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Head loss across a fitting | K-method, valve losses |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4f L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Darcy-Weisbach (straight pipe) | Frictional pressure drop |
| 6 | $C_v = Q \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ | Valve flow coefficient (liquid) | Valve sizing |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | Flow regime, friction factor |
| 8 | $W_{m2} = \pi b G y$ | Bolt load for gasket seating | Flange design |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho v A$ | Mass flow rate | Pipe sizing first step |
| 10 | $v_{opt} = \dfrac{Q}{(\pi/4) D^2}$ | Velocity check (1–3 m/s liquid, 15–30 m/s gas) | Economic pipe diameter |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery** — class 600 / 900 RTJ flanges with spiral-wound gaskets on hydrocracker reactors.
- **Pharma** — diaphragm valves with EPDM diaphragms on purified water loops (no dead-legs, no crevice).
- **Power plant** — gate valves on boiler feed, globe valves on bypass for throttling.
- **Offshore** — duplex / super-duplex pipes with weld-neck flanges for sour (H₂S) service per NACE MR0175.
- **Dairy / Food** — sanitary tri-clamp ferrules, EPDM or PTFE gaskets, butterfly valves for CIP.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are written out in full, with every algebraic transition made explicit. This is the style of derivation expected in the KTU University Examination's 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Schedule Number from Pressure and Stress

The Schedule Number is the *historical* (pre-1939) empirical correlation developed by the ASME to express pipe wall thickness in a way that scales linearly with pressure. It is derived from the basic thin-cylinder stress equation.

**Step 1:** Start with the hoop stress equation for a thin-walled cylindrical pipe:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for wall thickness $t$:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \sigma_h}
$$

**Step 3:** For safe design, replace $\sigma_h$ with the allowable stress $S$, and include a design factor. The Schedule Number is defined as:

$$
\text{Sch} = \frac{P \times 1000}{S}
$$

**Step 4:** For a given NPS, the OD ($D$) is fixed by ASME B36.10M. The wall thickness is then back-calculated:

$$
t = \frac{\text{Sch} \times D}{2000} \quad (\text{for } D \text{ in inches and } S \text{ in psi})
$$

**Interpretation:** Higher pressure ($P$) → higher stress demand → higher Schedule → thicker wall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Required Pipe Wall (ASME B31.3, Section 304.1.2)

**Problem:** A 6-inch NPS Sch 40 seamless carbon steel pipe (ASTM A106 Gr.B) carries steam at **4.0 MPa** and **260 °C**. The pipe is to be designed with a corrosion allowance of **1.5 mm**. Verify if the purchased wall is adequate. $E = 1.0$, $Y = 0.4$, $S = 137$ MPa (at 260 °C from Table A-1).

**Given:**
- $D = 168.28$ mm (OD of 6-inch NPS Sch 40)
- $t_{nom} = 7.11$ mm (Sch 40 wall)
- $P = 4.0$ MPa
- $S = 137$ MPa
- $E = 1.0$
- $Y = 0.4$
- $C = 1.5$ mm

**Step 1:** Compute the *pressure design* thickness (without corrosion allowance):

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)} = \frac{4.0 \times 168.28}{2 \left(137 \times 1.0 + 4.0 \times 0.4\right)}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the denominator:

$$
2(SE + PY) = 2(137 + 1.6) = 2 \times 138.6 = 277.2 \text{ MPa·mm/(MPa)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator:

$$
P \cdot D = 4.0 \times 168.28 = 673.12 \text{ MPa·mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide:

$$
t = \frac{673.12}{277.2} = 2.428 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Add the corrosion allowance:

$$
t_m = t + C = 2.428 + 1.5 = 3.928 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Account for mill tolerance (12.5 % negative tolerance on nominal wall):

$$
t_{purchase,\ min} = 7.11 \times (1 - 0.125) = 6.222 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Compare:

$$
t_{purchase,\ min} = 6.222 \text{ mm} \;\;\; > \;\;\; t_m = 3.928 \text{ mm} \;\;\; \therefore \text{ SAFE}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Sch 40 pipe is more than adequate for this service. We could *optimise* by selecting Sch 20S ($t_{nom} = 5.51$ mm), which would still satisfy $5.51 \times 0.875 = 4.82$ mm > $3.928$ mm. However, code of practice often requires a margin and the next standard schedule up.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop Across a Pipe + Fittings (Equivalent Length Method)

**Problem:** Water at 25 °C is pumped through a 4-inch Sch 40 steel pipe at a flow rate of **0.020 m³/s**. The line is 50 m long, contains 2 × 90° long-radius elbows, 1 × gate valve (fully open), and 1 × swing check valve. Compute the total pressure drop.

**Given:**
- $D_i$ (4-inch Sch 40) = **102.3 mm = 0.1023 m**
- $A = (\pi/4) D^2 = 0.008215$ m²
- $v = Q/A = 0.020 / 0.008215 = \mathbf{2.434}$ **m/s**
- $\rho = 997$ kg/m³, $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
- $L_{straight} = 50$ m

**Step 1:** Compute Reynolds number:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{997 \times 2.434 \times 0.1023}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.79 \times 10^5
$$

→ Turbulent flow.

**Step 2:** Compute roughness. Commercial steel $\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm. Relative roughness:

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045}{102.3} = 4.40 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the Colebrook or Haaland equation to find Darcy friction factor $f$. Using the **Swamee–Jain explicit approximation**:

$$
f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon}{3.7D} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}
$$

Compute the inside of the bracket:

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{3.7D} = \frac{0.045}{3.7 \times 102.3} = 1.189 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{(2.79 \times 10^5)^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{91878} = 6.247 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\text{sum} = 1.189 \times 10^{-4} + 6.247 \times 10^{-5} = 1.814 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(1.814 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.7416
$$

$$
f = \frac{0.25}{(-3.7416)^2} = \frac{0.25}{14.000} = 0.01786
$$

**Step 4:** Compute equivalent lengths of fittings (per Crane TP-410, $L_e/D$ values):

| Component | $L_e/D$ | $L_e$ (m) |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| 2 × 90° LR elbow | 30 × 2 = 60 | $60 \times 0.1023 = 6.138$ |
| 1 × Gate valve (open) | 8 | $8 \times 0.1023 = 0.818$ |
| 1 × Swing check | 100 | $100 \times 0.1023 = 10.23$ |
| **Total fitting $L_e$** | | **17.19** |

**Step 5:** Total equivalent length:

$$
L_{tot} = L_{straight} + L_{e,fittings} = 50 + 17.19 = 67.19 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply Darcy–Weisbach:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{4 f L_{tot}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

Compute the dynamic pressure term:

$$
\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{997 \times (2.434)^2}{2} = 2953 \text{ Pa}
$$

Compute the $4fL/D$ term:

$$
\frac{4 \times 0.01786 \times 67.19}{0.1023} = 46.91
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta P = 46.91 \times 2953 = 1.385 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = \mathbf{138.5 \text{ kPa}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fittings (≈17 m of equivalent pipe) contribute 17.19/67.19 = **25.6 %** of the total pressure drop. This is typical — fittings usually contribute 20–40 % of the total $\Delta P$ in a process line, which is why *low-loss* valves (gate, ball) are preferred over *high-loss* (globe) for service duty.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Valve Sizing Using $C_v$

**Problem:** A globe valve controls a flow of **20 m³/h of water** at a **50 kPa pressure drop** across the valve. Compute the required $C_v$. If the catalogue lists valves with $C_v$ ratings of 25, 38, 60, and 95, select the next standard size. $SG = 1.0$.

**Step 1:** Convert units. $Q$ must be in **GPM** for the $C_v$ formula:

$$
Q_{GPM} = \frac{20 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}{0.2271} = 88.07 \text{ GPM}
$$

> Note: 1 m³/h = 4.4029 GPM, so $Q = 20 \times 4.4029 = 88.06$ GPM.

**Step 2:** Convert pressure to **psi** (since $C_v$ is defined with $\Delta P$ in psi):

$$
\Delta P = \frac{50 \text{ kPa}}{6.895} = 7.252 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the $C_v$ formula:

$$
C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}} = 88.06 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{7.252}} = 88.06 \cdot 0.3712 = 32.69
$$

**Step 4:** Select the next standard valve size with $C_v$ rating ≥ 32.69. From the catalogue, select the **$C_v = 38$** valve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a real design, an *over-sizing factor* of 1.25–1.5 is applied to allow for future capacity and to avoid operating too close to the choked-flow limit.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Flange Bolt Load (Simplified Taylor Forge Method)

**Problem:** A Class 150 RF flange of NPS 4 carries steam at 1.5 MPa. Compute the minimum bolt load required to seat the gasket. Use a spiral-wound graphite gasket with $G$ = 130 mm (mean), $b$ = 12 mm, $y$ = 69 MPa.

**Step 1:** Recall the gasket seating bolt load equation:

$$
W_{m2} = \pi \cdot b \cdot G \cdot y
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
W_{m2} = \pi \times (0.012) \times (0.130) \times (69 \times 10^6)
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$
W_{m2} = 3.1416 \times 0.012 \times 0.130 \times 69 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the product stepwise:

$$
0.012 \times 0.130 = 1.56 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
1.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 69 \times 10^6 = 1.0764 \times 10^5
$$

$$
W_{m2} = 3.1416 \times 1.0764 \times 10^5 = \mathbf{3.382 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 338.2 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 5:** With this bolt load, the designer can now compute the *number* of bolts and the *bolt size* using ASME B16.5 flange tables. For a Class 150 NPS 4 flange, the standard bolt count is 8 × M16, each rated for ~80 kN of preload → 8 × 80 = 640 kN, which provides a *comfortable* margin of safety (~1.9×) over the seating load.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Selection Decision | Best Choice | Why |
|--------------------|-------------|-----|
| High-temp steam (≥ 425 °C) | Weld Neck flange + Spiral-wound graphite + Gate valve | Creep resistance, cyclic duty, low $\Delta P$ |
| Corrosive acid line | Lined pipe (PTFE/PP) + Lined valve + Lined gasket | Material compatibility |
| Slurry service | Pinch valve + rubber-lined pipe + eccentric reducer | Prevents solids accumulation |
| Cryogenic (LNG, –162 °C) | Stainless 304L + Ball valve + Spiral-wound PTFE | Low-temp toughness |
| Sanitary / Pharma | Diaphragm valve + Tri-clamp ferrule + EPDM gasket | No crevice, CIP-friendly |
| Boiler feed | Gate valve + Weld Neck + Graphite gasket | High P, high T, on/off duty |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Instructions:** Answer in 3–4 sentences maximum. Each carries **3 marks** as per KTU 2024 Part A.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between a **gate valve** and a **globe valve** in terms of (i) flow direction change, (ii) pressure drop when fully open, and (iii) suitability for throttling service.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| Flow path | Straight, unobstructed | Z-shaped, tortuous |
| ΔP fully open | Very low (≈ 0.05–0.10 bar) | High (≈ 8–10× gate) |
| Throttling | **Poor** — seat erodes in partially open position | **Excellent** — designed for it |

A gate valve is a *full-bore isolation* valve, while a globe valve is a *throttling* valve. Globe valves cause ~10× the pressure drop of gate valves but provide *precise* flow control and *tight* shutoff.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is a **weld-neck (WN) flange** preferred over a **slip-on (SO) flange** for high-pressure, high-temperature service?

**Model Answer:**

A weld-neck flange has a *long tapered hub* that is butt-welded to the pipe, providing:
1. **Gradual stress transition** from the flange to the pipe wall — reduces stress concentration at the weld.
2. **Better fatigue life** under thermal cycling.
3. **Higher pressure–temperature rating** for the same pressure class.

A slip-on flange has a *fillet weld* that is weaker and is restricted to low-pressure non-critical service (e.g., utility water, firewater). The fillet weld cannot be radiographed and is prone to crevice corrosion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. *Internal choice* means you answer **either** Q-A or Q-B from the same module.

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** A 6-inch NPS Sch 40 carbon steel pipe (ASTM A106 Gr.B) carries saturated steam at 3.5 MPa gauge and 245 °C. Verify whether the purchased wall thickness is adequate per ASME B31.3. Use: $E = 1.0$, $Y = 0.4$, $S$ at 245 °C = 138 MPa, $C = 2.0$ mm, mill tolerance = 12.5 %.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, classify the different types of **flanges** and **gaskets** used in process piping, indicating their typical service applications.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Identify pipe dimensions (4-inch NPS Sch 40 CS, per ASME B36.10M):**
- $D_{out} = 168.28$ mm
- $t_{nom} = 7.11$ mm
- Pipe ID = 168.28 − 2(7.11) = 154.06 mm

**Step 2 — Apply the B31.3 pressure design equation:**

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
t = \frac{3.5 \times 168.28}{2(138 \times 1.0 + 3.5 \times 0.4)} = \frac{589.0}{2 \times 139.4} = \frac{589.0}{278.8} = 2.113 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$
t_m = 2.113 + 2.0 = 4.113 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 12.5 % negative mill tolerance on nominal wall:**

$$
t_{effective} = 7.11 \times 0.875 = 6.221 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$
t_{effective} = 6.221 \text{ mm} \;\; > \;\; t_m = 4.113 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow \text{ SAFE}
$$

> Conclusion: The Sch 40 pipe is *adequate*; the design margin is $\dfrac{6.221 - 4.113}{4.113} \times 100\% \approx 51\%$, indicating significant over-design. The designer could *optimise* by selecting Sch 20S ($t = 5.51$ mm) which would still satisfy the criterion.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

Refer to the **classification tree of flanges** and **gasket selection flowchart** in Section 4 of these notes. The student should:

1. Draw the **flange tree** (WN, SO, SW, THD, LJ, BL) with one-line applications.
2. Draw the **gasket tree** (Soft: NAF, Rubber, PTFE; Semi-metallic: Spiral-wound, Kammprofile; Metallic: RTJ) with temperature limits.
3. Provide a **3–4-line application note** for each: e.g., *WN + Spiral-wound graphite* for refinery hydrocracker at 425 °C; *SO + NAF* for utility water at 30 °C; *RTJ + soft iron* for high-pressure steam header at 8 MPa.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** A centrifugal pump discharges 30 m³/h of water at 25 °C through a 3-inch Sch 40 steel pipe. The line is 80 m long and contains 3 × 90° LR elbows, 1 × gate valve, and 1 × swing check valve. Compute the total pressure drop and the equivalent length of the fittings. Use $f = 0.020$ and $\rho = 997$ kg/m³.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A globe valve is selected to throttle the flow of 50 m³/h of water with a design pressure drop of 80 kPa. Calculate the required $C_v$ and choose a standard valve from the catalogue sizes: 40, 60, 95, 130. Comment on the throttling characteristic.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Step 1 — Pipe dimensions (3-inch Sch 40):**
- $D_i = 77.93$ mm = 0.07793 m
- $A = \pi/4 \times (0.07793)^2 = 4.769 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

**Step 2 — Velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{30/3600}{4.769 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.748 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total equivalent length of fittings (Crane TP-410):**

| Fitting | $L_e/D$ | $L_e$ (m) |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| 3 × 90° LR elbow | $3 \times 30 = 90$ | $90 \times 0.07793 = 7.014$ |
| 1 × Gate valve | 8 | $8 \times 0.07793 = 0.623$ |
| 1 × Swing check | 100 | $100 \times 0.07793 = 7.793$ |
| **Total** | | **15.43 m** |

**Step 4 — Total length including straight pipe:**

$$
L_{tot} = 80 + 15.43 = 95.43 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Darcy–Weisbach equation:**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{4 f L_{tot}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 95.43}{0.07793} \times \frac{997 \times (1.748)^2}{2}
$$

Compute each factor:

$$
\frac{4 f L}{D} = \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 95.43}{0.07793} = 97.97
$$

$$
\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{997 \times 3.055}{2} = 1522.9 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 97.97 \times 1522.9 = 1.492 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = \mathbf{149.2 \text{ kPa}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The pump must generate at least 1.5 bar of head in addition to system static head and any control valve drop.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Convert to GPM:**
- $Q = 50$ m³/h × 4.4029 = **220.15 GPM**

**Step 2 — Convert ΔP to psi:**
- $\Delta P = 80$ kPa ÷ 6.895 = **11.60 psi**

**Step 3 — Compute $C_v$:**

$$
C_v = Q \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}} = 220.15 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{11.60}} = 220.15 \times 0.2937 = 64.66
$$

**Step 4 — Select next standard size:**
- $C_v$ required = 64.66 → choose **$C_v = 95$** valve (next catalogue size up).
- Over-sizing factor = 95 / 64.66 = 1.47 — acceptable.

**Step 5 — Comment on throttling characteristic:**
- A globe valve has an **equal-percentage** inherent characteristic (inherent), but the *installed* characteristic becomes more *linear* when the system has high piping friction.
- For accurate control, an equal-percentage trim is preferred when the design point is at 30–70 % valve travel.
- Globe valves with contoured plugs give **better rangeability** (50:1 or higher) compared to ball valves (which have quick-opening characteristics).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the mill tolerance** (12.5 %) when verifying wall thickness → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** in $C_v$ calculations (using m³/h and kPa without converting to GPM and psi) → loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Not identifying fittings separately** from straight pipe in pressure-drop problems — the question often asks for "**total** ΔP" which must include fittings.
> 4. **Confusing NPS with ID** — NPS 4 does *not* mean 4-inch inside diameter. The OD is 4.5 inches for NPS 4.
> 5. **Confusing flange *Class* with pressure** — Class 300 does not mean 300 psi; at 38 °C it is ~740 psi, but at 425 °C it is only ~475 psi. Always read the P-T table.
> 6. **Selecting spiral-wound for a low-pressure utility line** — wastes money. Match the gasket to the *weakest* of pressure, temperature, and fluid.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Pipes** are sized by NPS and Schedule; wall thickness verified by ASME B31.3 equation with corrosion + mill tolerance.
- [x] **Schedule Number** is a historical correlation: $\text{Sch} = (P \times 1000) / S$.
- [x] **Fittings** cause additional ΔP, captured by *equivalent length* ($L_e = K \times D / f$) using $L_e/D$ values from Crane TP-410.
- [x] **Flanges** are classified into 6 types: WN, SO, SW, THD, LJ, BL. WN is the strongest.
- [x] **Pressure–Temperature ratings** of flanges drop as temperature rises (always consult ASME B16.5 Table 2-1.1).
- [x] **Gaskets** are classified as soft (NAF, rubber, PTFE), semi-metallic (spiral-wound, kammprofile), and metallic (RTJ).
- [x] **Spiral-wound graphite** is the *workhorse* gasket for refinery and petrochemical service.
- [x] **Valves** are classified by *function*: isolation (gate/ball/butterfly), throttling (globe/needle), non-return (swing/lift/dual-plate check), relief (safety/relief/rupture disc).
- [x] **$C_v$ formula** for liquids: $C_v = Q\sqrt{SG/\Delta P}$, with $Q$ in GPM and $\Delta P$ in psi.
- [x] **Globe valve** has the **highest ΔP** (≈ 10× gate) but best throttling. **Gate/ball** have lowest ΔP and are for isolation.
- [x] **Darcy–Weisbach** with $L_{tot} = L_{straight} + \sum L_{e,fittings}$ gives total pressure drop.
- [x] **Bolt load** for gasket seating: $W_{m2} = \pi b G y$, used to design flange bolt-up.
- [x] **Sanitary service** demands diaphragm valves, tri-clamp ferrules, EPDM gaskets — no crevice, no dead-legs.
- [x] **Materials matter** — ASTM grade selection depends on temperature, pressure, and corrosion; consult ASME B31.3 tables A-1, A-2.
- [x] **Standards to remember**: ASME B31.3, B16.5, B16.9, B16.34, MSS-SP-25, API 600/602/6D, NACE MR0175.
